descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
html:
Musi for C++ Developers

C++17 is the comparison point for this guide. C++ developers often bring RAII, classes, templates, namespaces, std::optional, std::variant, exceptions, headers, and native ABI work. Musi keeps those jobs recognizable while making values, data shape, and outside work explicit.

C++ usually starts with a small function and a call:

auto total(const int base_price, const int fee) -> int {
    return base_price + fee;
}

const auto answer = total(1200, 45);

Musi gives the function the same inputs and result, then lets the body expression produce the value.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Reading path

Read the guide from everyday C++ toward deeper translation points:

  1. values, functions, blocks, and mutation;
  2. records, collections, pointers, optional values, results, and effects;
  3. variants, templates, classes, and receiver calls;
  4. packages, tests, unsafe code, and native FFI boundaries.
summaryHtml: Start from C++17 habits, then read equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, package, and FFI shapes.

title: Values, Functions, and Expressions
description: Translate C++17 functions, expression habits, and named arguments into Musi expression-oriented bindings.
summary: C++ functions and call sites map to Musi let functions, expression bodies, and named calls.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 functions, expression habits, and named arguments into Musi expression-oriented bindings.
html:
Values, Functions, and Expressions

C++17 often returns values through explicit return statements:

auto total(const int base_price, const int fee) -> int {
    return base_price + fee;
}

const auto answer = total(1200, 45);

Musi keeps the same input and output types, but the body is the produced value.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

C++ call sites depend on parameter order:

auto render(const int port, const bool secure) -> int {
    return port;
}

const auto selected = render(8080, true);

Musi can name arguments where the call benefits from visible roles.

let render (port : Int, secure : Bool) : Int := port;

let selected := render(port := 8080, secure := 0 = 0);
selected;
summaryHtml: C++ functions and call sites map to Musi let functions, expression bodies, and named calls.

title: Blocks and Control Flow
description: Translate C++17 blocks, branching, switches, and loops into Musi block expressions, match, and recursion.
summary: Use blocks as values and model small state-machine loops with recursion.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 blocks, branching, switches, and loops into Musi block expressions, match, and recursion.
html:
Blocks and Control Flow

C++17 blocks group statements and return explicitly when a value leaves a function:

auto invoice_total() -> int {
    const auto base_price = 1200;
    const auto fee = 45;
    return base_price + fee;
}

Musi blocks can produce the final value directly.

let invoiceTotal () : Int :=
  (
    let basePrice := 1200;
    let fee := 45;
    basePrice + fee
  );

invoiceTotal();

C++ loops commonly mutate loop state:

auto total_seats(const std::vector<int>& groups) -> int {
    auto seats = 0;
    for (const auto group : groups) {
        seats += group * 4;
    }
    return seats;
}

Musi uses recursion when the loop is a small state machine.

let rec totalSeats (remaining : Int, seats : Int) : Int :=
  match remaining (
  | 0 => seats
  | _ => totalSeats(remaining - 1, seats + 4)
  );

totalSeats(3, 0);

For collections, prefer library traversal and pipelines. For changing counters or accumulators, make state explicit in recursive parameters.
summaryHtml: Use blocks as values and model small state-machine loops with recursion.

title: Variables and Mutation
description: Translate C++17 const and mutable locals into Musi stable bindings and explicit mut values.
summary: C++ mutable locals map to explicit Musi mut values or fresh derived names.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 const and mutable locals into Musi stable bindings and explicit mut values.
html:
Variables and Mutation

C++ separates stable values from changing locals with const:

auto visits = 0;
visits = visits + 1;

const auto next_visits = visits + 1;

Musi flips the default and marks only the changing value.

let visits := mut 0;
visits := visits + 1;

let nextVisits := visits + 1;
nextVisits;

When a value is only a derived step, C++ often names another local:

const auto base_price = 1200;
const auto total = base_price + 45;

Musi uses the same fresh-name style without marking either binding mutable.

let basePrice := 1200;
let total := basePrice + 45;
total;
summaryHtml: C++ mutable locals map to explicit Musi mut values or fresh derived names.

title: Structs, Classes, and Records
description: Translate C++17 structs and simple classes into Musi records and record updates.
summary: C++ structs map to Musi records with named fields and explicit updates.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 structs and simple classes into Musi records and record updates.
html:
Structs, Classes, and Records

C++17 records simple data with structs or small classes:

struct Endpoint {
    std::string host;
    int port;
    bool secure;
};

const auto local = Endpoint{"localhost", 8080, false};
auto public_endpoint = local;
public_endpoint.host = "api.example.com";
public_endpoint.secure = true;

const auto port = public_endpoint.port;

Musi keeps the record shape local and uses record update syntax.

let Endpoint := data {
  host : String;
  port : Int;
  secure : Bool;
};

let local := {
  host := "localhost",
  port := 8080,
  secure := 0 = 1
};

let publicEndpoint := { ...local, host := "api.example.com", secure := 0 = 0 };
publicEndpoint.port;
summaryHtml: C++ structs map to Musi records with named fields and explicit updates.

title: Arrays, Pointers, and Slices
description: Translate C++17 containers, arrays, iterators, and pointer work into Musi collections and explicit FFI pointers.
summary: Use Musi collections for safe data and reserve raw pointer work for FFI boundaries.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 containers, arrays, iterators, and pointer work into Musi collections and explicit FFI pointers.
html:
Arrays, Pointers, and Slices

C++17 often uses standard containers for growable values:

auto ports = std::vector<int>{3000, 8080};
ports.push_back(9000);

const auto visible = ports;

Musi keeps ordinary collections separate from native pointer work.

let iter := import "@std/iter";

let ports := [3000, 8080];
let visible := ports
  |> iter.append[Int](9000)
  |> iter.collect[Int]();
visible;
summaryHtml: Use Musi collections for safe data and reserve raw pointer work for FFI boundaries.

title: Null, Option, and Result
description: Translate C++17 null pointers, optional values, and fallible returns into Musi Option and Result values.
summary: Use Option for expected absence and Result for fallible values.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 null pointers, optional values, and fallible returns into Musi Option and Result values.
html:
Null, Option, and Result

C++17 uses std::optional when absence is expected:

auto lookup_port(const std::string_view name) -> std::optional<int> {
    if (name == "admin") {
        return 9000;
    }
    return std::nullopt;
}

const auto port = lookup_port("web").value_or(8080);

Musi uses Option when absence is part of the value.

let option := import "@std/option";

let lookupPort (name : String) : option.Option[Int] :=
  match name (
  | "admin" => option.some[Int](9000)
  | _ => option.none[Int]()
  );

let port := lookupPort("web")
  |> option.unwrapOr[Int](8080);
port;

C++17 projects often define a small result object when exceptions are not the right fit:

struct ParseResult {
    bool ok;
    int port;
    std::string error;
};

Musi uses Result when failure is part of the returned value.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("invalid port")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;
summaryHtml: Use Option for expected absence and Result for fallible values.

title: Errors, Results, and Effects
description: Translate C++17 exceptions, result objects, and host interaction into Musi Result values and effect boundaries.
summary: Keep expected failure as data and outside work behind effectful APIs.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 exceptions, result objects, and host interaction into Musi Result values and effect boundaries.
html:
Errors, Results, and Effects

C++17 code often builds a small result object when exceptions are not the right fit:

struct ParseResult {
    bool ok;
    int port;
    std::string error;
};

auto parse_port(const std::string_view text) -> ParseResult {
    if (text == "8080") {
        return {true, 8080, ""};
    }
    return {false, 0, "parse error"};
}

Musi keeps expected failure in the returned value.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("parse error")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;

C++ host interaction usually calls library APIs directly:

auto name = std::string{};
std::cout << "name> ";
std::getline(std::cin, name);
std::cout << name << '\n';

Musi keeps console work at effectful boundaries.

let io := import "@std/io";

let name := io.promptTrimmed("name> ");
io.writeLine(name);
summaryHtml: Keep expected failure as data and outside work behind effectful APIs.

title: Enums, Variants, and Patterns
description: Translate C++17 enum class and std::variant habits into Musi data variants and match.
summary: Use data variants when alternatives carry distinct shapes.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 enum class and std::variant habits into Musi data variants and match.
html:
Enums, Variants, and Patterns

C++17 can model alternatives with std::variant and a visitor:

struct Configured {
    int port;
};

struct Default {};

using Port = std::variant<Configured, Default>;

const auto selected = Port{Configured{8080}};
const auto port = std::visit([](const auto& state) -> int {
    using State = std::decay_t<decltype(state)>;
    if constexpr (std::is_same_v<State, Configured>) {
        return state.port;
    }
    return 3000;
}, selected);

Musi makes alternatives data variants and matches them directly.

let TaskState := data {
  | Waiting
  | Running(id : Int)
  | Done(code : Int)
};

let state : TaskState := .Running(id := 42);
match state (
| .Running(id) => id
| .Waiting => 0
| .Done(code) => code
);
summaryHtml: Use data variants when alternatives carry distinct shapes.

title: Templates, Concepts, Classes, and Laws
description: Translate C++17 templates and behavior constraints into Musi type parameters, classes, and laws.
summary: Use type parameters for reusable values and classes for reusable behavior.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 templates and behavior constraints into Musi type parameters, classes, and laws.
html:
Templates, Concepts, Classes, and Laws

C++17 templates express reusable value-level operations:

template <class T>
auto identity(T input) -> T {
    return input;
}

const auto answer = identity(21);

Musi uses type parameters for reusable values.

let identity[T] (input : T) : T := input;

let port := identity[Int](8080);
port;

C++ expresses required behavior through templates, traits, and concepts in later standards:

template <class T>
auto wheels(const T& vehicle) -> int {
    return vehicle.wheels();
}

Musi classes state the required operations and laws directly.

let Vehicle[T] := class {
  let wheels(self : T) : Int;
  law atLeastFourWheels(vehicle : T) := vehicle.wheels() >= 4;
};

let Car := data {
  | Car
};

let carVehicle := instance Vehicle[Car] {
  let wheels(self : Car) : Int := 4;
};
summaryHtml: Use type parameters for reusable values and classes for reusable behavior.

title: Methods and Receiver Calls
description: Translate C++17 methods into Musi functions and receiver-style calls where they improve reading.
summary: Keep functions primary and use receiver calls for left-to-right transformations.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 methods into Musi functions and receiver-style calls where they improve reading.
html:
Methods and Receiver Calls

C++17 attaches receiver behavior to a class or struct:

struct Port {
    int port;

    auto with_offset(const int offset) const -> int {
        return port + offset;
    }
};

const auto selected = Port{8080}.with_offset(100);

Musi keeps functions primary while allowing receiver-style calls.

let (port : Int).withOffset (offset : Int) : Int := port + offset;

let publicPort := 8080.withOffset(1);
publicPort;
summaryHtml: Keep functions primary and use receiver calls for left-to-right transformations.

title: Headers, Modules, and Packages
description: Translate C++17 headers, namespaces, and source organization into Musi exports and package imports.
summary: Expose package-facing values with export and import dependencies by path.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 headers, namespaces, and source organization into Musi exports and package imports.
html:
Headers, Namespaces, and Packages

C++17 exposes package-facing declarations through headers:

#pragma once

namespace ports {
auto default_port() -> int;
}

Musi exports the package-facing value directly.

export let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

C++ source files include headers and call through namespaces:

#include "ports.hpp"

auto selected_port() -> int {
    return ports::default_port();
}

Musi imports package values directly.

let Ports := import "./ports";

let port := Ports.defaultPort();
port;
summaryHtml: Expose package-facing values with export and import dependencies by path.

title: Testing and Tooling
description: Translate C++17 test framework conventions into Musi package tests and std testing helpers.
summary: Keep tests beside the package and run them through musi test.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 test framework conventions into Musi package tests and std testing helpers.
html:
Testing and Tooling

C++17 tests often call ordinary functions through a framework or assert:

#include <cassert>

auto total(const int base_price, const int fee) -> int {
    return base_price + fee;
}

auto test_total() -> void {
    assert(total(1200, 45) == 1245);
}

Musi keeps checks beside the package with @std/testing.

let testing := import "@std/testing";

testing.describe("ports");
testing.it("default port", testing.equal[Int](8080, 8080));
testing.endDescribe();
summaryHtml: Keep tests beside the package and run them through musi test.

title: Unsafe, FFI, and Native Boundaries
description: Translate C++17 native wrappers and pointer operations into Musi foreign declarations and unsafe wrappers.
summary: Keep raw native calls and pointer work behind narrow unsafe boundaries.
descriptionHtml: Translate C++17 native wrappers and pointer operations into Musi foreign declarations and unsafe wrappers.
html:
Unsafe, FFI, and Native Boundaries

C++17 crosses stable ABI boundaries through extern "C" wrappers:

extern "C" auto puts(const char* message) -> int;

extern "C" auto announce(const char* message) -> int {
    return puts(message);
}

Musi marks native boundaries with foreign and unsafe.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

foreign "c" let puts (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt;

export let announce (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt :=
  unsafe { puts(message); };

C++ pointer arithmetic is ordinary syntax:

const auto* pointer = static_cast<const int*>(nullptr);
const auto* same_pointer = pointer + 0;
const auto is_null = same_pointer == nullptr;

Musi routes raw pointer work through @std/ffi helpers.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

let pointer := ffi.ptr.null[Int]();
let samePointer := unsafe { ffi.ptr.offset[Int](pointer, 0); };
ffi.ptr.isNull[Int](samePointer);
summaryHtml: Keep raw native calls and pointer work behind narrow unsafe boundaries.

title: Overview
description: Translate .NET 8.0 and C# 12.0 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
summary: Start from .NET 8.0 and C# 12.0 habits, then read the equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.
descriptionHtml: Translate .NET 8.0 and C# 12.0 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
html:
.NET 8.0 and C# 12.0 are the comparison points for this guide. Each page starts with C# code, then shows Musi code with the same names and the same job.

C# readers often bring methods, classes, records, nullable references, exceptions, async tasks, interfaces, extension methods, namespaces, and native interop. Musi keeps those jobs recognizable while making values, effects, and data shape explicit.

First translation

C# can write a small expression-bodied method:

static int Total(int basePrice, int fee) => basePrice + fee;

var answer = Total(1200, 45);
answer;

Musi writes the same calculation as a let function. The final expression leaves the value.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Read it like a service counter: arguments arrive, one expression calculates the receipt, and the final value leaves the counter.

Reading path

  • Values, Methods, and Expressions
  • Blocks and Control Flow
  • Variables and Mutation
  • Records, Classes, and Objects
  • Collections, LINQ, and Pipelines
  • Null, Option, and Result
  • Exceptions, Results, and Effects
  • Unions and Pattern Matching
  • Generics, Interfaces, and Laws
  • Extension Methods and Calls
  • Namespaces, Modules, and Packages
  • Testing and Tooling
  • Unsafe, Interop, and FFI

C# habits that transfer

  • name domain types clearly
  • keep boundary work visible
  • use static types to explain values
  • keep tests close to behavior
  • use small functions when a calculation has a name

C# habits to translate

  • return becomes the final expression
  • records and classes split into records, data, classes, and instances by job
  • nullable values become Option
  • ordinary recoverable exceptions become Result
  • async and outside work become effects or stdlib runtime helpers
  • interfaces become classes, instances, and laws when behavior needs rules
  • native interop stays behind foreign and unsafe
summaryHtml: Start from .NET 8.0 and C# 12.0 habits, then read the equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.

title: Values, Methods, and Expressions
description: Translate C# methods, expression-bodied members, and return into Musi let functions and final expressions.
summary: Use let functions and final expressions where C# uses methods and return.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# methods, expression-bodied members, and return into Musi let functions and final expressions.
html:
C# methods name work. Expression-bodied methods make a simple calculation compact:

static int Total(int basePrice, int fee) => basePrice + fee;

var answer = Total(1200, 45);
answer;

Musi uses let to name functions. The body can be the expression itself.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

No return is needed for this shape. The expression after := is the value.

Named arguments

C# named arguments make call sites clearer:

static int Render(int port, bool secure) => port;

var selected = Render(port: 8080, secure: true);
selected;

Musi uses named arguments for the same reading job.

let render (port : Int, secure : Bool) : Int := port;

let selected := render(port := 8080, secure := 0 = 0);
selected;

Use named calls when two adjacent values could be confused.
summaryHtml: Use let functions and final expressions where C# uses methods and return.

title: Blocks and Control Flow
description: Translate C# statement blocks, switch expressions, and loops into Musi blocks, match arms, and recursion.
summary: Use expression blocks, match arms, and let rec for repeated work.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# statement blocks, switch expressions, and loops into Musi blocks, match arms, and recursion.
html:
C# statement blocks often collect local setup before a return:

static int InvoiceTotal()
{
    var basePrice = 1200;
    var fee = 45;
    return basePrice + fee;
}

InvoiceTotal();

Musi blocks produce a value. The final expression is what leaves the block.

let invoiceTotal () : Int :=
  (
    let basePrice := 1200;
    let fee := 45;
    basePrice + fee
  );

invoiceTotal();

Think of the block as a workbench. Local names stay on the bench; the final expression is the finished part.

Repetition

C# loops can update local state:

static int TotalSeats(int rounds)
{
    var seats = 0;
    for (var remaining = rounds; remaining > 0; remaining -= 1)
    {
        seats += 4;
    }
    return seats;
}

Musi gives repeated work a name with let rec.

let rec totalSeats (remaining : Int, seats : Int) : Int :=
  match remaining (
  | 0 => seats
  | _ => totalSeats(remaining - 1, seats + 4)
  );

totalSeats(3, 0);

The match arms describe the two roads: stop, or continue with less remaining work.
summaryHtml: Use expression blocks, match arms, and let rec for repeated work.

title: Variables and Mutation
description: Translate C# locals, var, and assignment into Musi names, mut values, and fresh bindings.
summary: Use mut for real state changes and fresh names for ordinary derived values.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# locals, var, and assignment into Musi names, mut values, and fresh bindings.
html:
C# local variables can be reassigned:

var visits = 0;
visits += 1;

var nextVisits = visits + 1;
nextVisits;

Musi marks the value that can change with mut.

let visits := mut 0;
visits := visits + 1;

let nextVisits := visits + 1;
nextVisits;

Use mut like a visible dial on a machine. Readers know which dial can move.

Fresh values

C# often uses var for derived values:

var basePrice = 1200;
var total = basePrice + 45;
total;

Musi fresh bindings read the same way.

let basePrice := 1200;
let total := basePrice + 45;
total;

Use fresh names when a later value is another fact, not a state change.
summaryHtml: Use mut for real state changes and fresh names for ordinary derived values.

title: Records, Classes, and Objects
description: Translate C# records, classes, and object updates into Musi data definitions and records.
summary: Use data definitions for named shapes and records for named field values.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# records, classes, and object updates into Musi data definitions and records.
html:
C# records give a named shape and a copy-update form:

public sealed record Endpoint(string Host, int Port, bool Secure);

var local = new Endpoint("localhost", 8080, false);
var publicEndpoint = local with { Host = "api.example.com", Secure = true };
publicEndpoint.Port;

Musi names the shape with data and updates field values with record spread.

let Endpoint := data {
  host : String;
  port : Int;
  secure : Bool;
};

let local := {
  host := "localhost",
  port := 8080,
  secure := 0 = 1
};

let publicEndpoint := { ...local, host := "api.example.com", secure := 0 = 0 };
publicEndpoint.port;

Use records like a badge: each field name says what role the value plays.

Class habit

C# classes often mix identity, mutation, and behavior. In Musi, choose the smallest shape that matches the job: a record for fields, a data variant for cases, and a class plus instance for shared behavior.
summaryHtml: Use data definitions for named shapes and records for named field values.

title: Collections, LINQ, and Pipelines
description: Translate C# collection and LINQ habits into Musi arrays, stdlib helpers, and pipelines.
summary: Use arrays and pipeline-first stdlib helpers for visible collection flow.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# collection and LINQ habits into Musi arrays, stdlib helpers, and pipelines.
html:
C# 12.0 collection expressions make small arrays direct:

int[] ports = [3000, 8080];
int[] visible = [.. ports, 9000];
visible;

Musi arrays use [], and pipelines keep transformations left to right.

let iter := import "@std/iter";

let ports := [3000, 8080];
let visible := ports
  |> iter.append[Int](9000)
  |> iter.collect[Int]();
visible;

Read the pipeline like a conveyor: the collection enters on the left, each helper does one step, and the final value leaves on the right.

LINQ habit

LINQ chains are useful because readers can follow each operation. Musi pipelines keep that same shape, with the data flow visible at the beginning of each line.
summaryHtml: Use arrays and pipeline-first stdlib helpers for visible collection flow.

title: Null, Option, and Result
description: Translate C# nullable reference habits into Musi Option and Result values.
summary: Use Option for maybe-present values and Result for success-or-error data.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# nullable reference habits into Musi Option and Result values.
html:
C# nullable references make absence visible in the type:

static int? LookupPort(string name) =>
    name == "admin" ? 9000 : null;

var port = LookupPort("web") ?? 8080;
port;

Musi uses Option to make the same absence explicit.

let option := import "@std/option";

let lookupPort (name : String) : option.Option[Int] :=
  match name (
  | "admin" => option.some[Int](9000)
  | _ => option.none[Int]()
  );

let port := lookupPort("web")
  |> option.unwrapOr[Int](8080);
port;

The function reports whether a value exists. The caller chooses the fallback.

Result for ordinary failure

When a C# API would return a success value or an error value, Musi can use Result.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("invalid port")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;

Use Option for maybe-present values. Use Result when the failure carries information.
summaryHtml: Use Option for maybe-present values and Result for success-or-error data.

title: Exceptions, Results, and Effects
description: Translate C# exceptions, async tasks, and outside work into Musi Result values and effectful boundaries.
summary: Use Result for recoverable failure and effects or stdlib helpers for requested outside work.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# exceptions, async tasks, and outside work into Musi Result values and effectful boundaries.
html:
C# exceptions can move failure out of the return path:

static int ParsePort(string text)
{
    try
    {
        return int.Parse(text);
    }
    catch (FormatException)
    {
        return 3000;
    }
}

Musi keeps ordinary failure in a Result value.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("format error")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;

Use this when bad input is part of normal use, not a crash.

Async and outside work

C# Task, async, I/O, timers, and process calls reach outside a pure calculation. Musi keeps those edges visible through stdlib modules and effects.

let io := import "@std/io";

let name := io.promptTrimmed("name> ");
io.writeLine(name);

The import names the outside capability. The call site shows where input and output happen.
summaryHtml: Use Result for recoverable failure and effects or stdlib helpers for requested outside work.

title: Unions and Pattern Matching
description: Translate C# switch expressions and inheritance-based cases into Musi data variants and match arms.
summary: Use data variants when each case has a name and a different payload.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# switch expressions and inheritance-based cases into Musi data variants and match arms.
html:
C# often models cases with a base type and derived records:

abstract record TaskState;
sealed record Waiting() : TaskState;
sealed record Running(int Id) : TaskState;
sealed record Done(int Code) : TaskState;

TaskState state = new Running(42);
var selected = state switch
{
    Running(var id) => id,
    Waiting => 0,
    Done(var code) => code,
};

Musi names those cases directly in one data definition.

let TaskState := data {
  | Waiting
  | Running(id : Int)
  | Done(code : Int)
};

let state : TaskState := .Running(id := 42);
match state (
| .Running(id) => id
| .Waiting => 0
| .Done(code) => code
);

Use variants when the set of cases matters more than object inheritance.
summaryHtml: Use data variants when each case has a name and a different payload.

title: Generics, Interfaces, and Laws
description: Translate C# generics, interfaces, and constraints into Musi type parameters, classes, instances, and laws.
summary: Use generics for reusable shape and classes with laws for shared behavior with rules.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# generics, interfaces, and constraints into Musi type parameters, classes, instances, and laws.
html:
C# generics let one function keep the same shape for many types:

static T Identity<T>(T input) => input;

var port = Identity<int>(8080);
port;

Musi writes type parameters beside the function name.

let identity[T] (input : T) : T := input;

let port := identity[Int](8080);
port;

The function promises to return the same type it receives.

Interfaces and laws

C# interfaces name behavior. Musi classes name behavior too, and laws can name rules that valid instances should satisfy.

public interface IVehicle
{
    int Wheels { get; }
}

Musi separates the behavior from the instance that provides it.

let Vehicle[T] := class {
  let wheels(self : T) : Int;
  law atLeastFourWheels(vehicle : T) := vehicle.wheels() >= 4;
};

let Car := data {
  | Car
};

let carVehicle := instance Vehicle[Car] {
  let wheels(self : Car) : Int := 4;
};

A car is a vehicle, and the law says what this model expects from valid vehicle instances.
summaryHtml: Use generics for reusable shape and classes with laws for shared behavior with rules.

title: Extension Methods and Calls
description: Translate C# extension method call habits into Musi receiver methods and ordinary calls.
summary: Use receiver methods when a call should read from the value outward.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# extension method call habits into Musi receiver methods and ordinary calls.
html:
C# extension methods let a static function read like a method on the receiver:

public static class PortExtensions
{
    public static int WithOffset(this int port, int offset) => port + offset;
}

var publicPort = 8080.WithOffset(1);
publicPort;

Musi receiver methods serve the same reading goal.

let (port : Int).withOffset (offset : Int) : Int := port + offset;

let publicPort := 8080.withOffset(1);
publicPort;

Use a receiver method when the left-side value is the thing being described.

Ordinary calls still fit

Not every helper should be a method. If the operation belongs to a module or package rather than one receiver value, keep it as a plain function call.
summaryHtml: Use receiver methods when a call should read from the value outward.

title: Namespaces, Modules, and Packages
description: Translate C# namespaces, files, and NuGet-style package habits into Musi exports, imports, and package paths.
summary: Use export for public names and import for package or relative module boundaries.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# namespaces, files, and NuGet-style package habits into Musi exports, imports, and package paths.
html:
C# namespaces and projects organize public APIs:

namespace Ports;

public static class Defaults
{
    public static int DefaultPort() => 8080;
}

Musi marks public names with export.

export let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

Another file imports the module and calls the exported function.

using Ports;

var port = Defaults.DefaultPort();
port;

Musi imports the module value and reads exported names from it.

let Defaults := import "./defaults";

let port := Defaults.defaultPort();
port;

Package imports such as @std/io are for shared packages. Relative imports such as ./ports are for nearby project files.
summaryHtml: Use export for public names and import for package or relative module boundaries.

title: Testing and Tooling
description: Translate C# test habits into Musi std testing helpers and command-line checks.
summary: Use @std/testing to keep tests close to behavior.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# test habits into Musi std testing helpers and command-line checks.
html:
C# tests commonly name a behavior and assert the result:

[Fact]
public void DefaultPortIsHttpAlt()
{
    Assert.Equal(8080, DefaultPort());
}

Musi uses @std/testing for named suites and cases.

let testing := import "@std/testing";

let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

export let test () :=
  (
    testing.describe("ports");
    testing.it("default port is http alt", testing.toBe(defaultPort(), 8080));
    testing.endDescribe()
  );

A test should read like a receipt: name the behavior, run the check, and leave a pass or fail value.

Tooling habit

.NET projects commonly separate restore, build, test, and format steps. Musi projects should keep the same habit: use commands that make syntax, package shape, and test behavior visible.
summaryHtml: Use @std/testing to keep tests close to behavior.

title: Unsafe, Interop, and FFI
description: Translate C# unsafe code and P/Invoke habits into Musi foreign declarations, Ptr values, and unsafe blocks.
summary: Keep native calls explicit with foreign declarations, @std/ffi types, and unsafe blocks.
descriptionHtml: Translate C# unsafe code and P/Invoke habits into Musi foreign declarations, Ptr values, and unsafe blocks.
html:
C# can cross native boundaries with P/Invoke and unsafe code:

using System.Runtime.InteropServices;

[LibraryImport("c")]
internal static partial int puts(IntPtr message);

Musi declares the foreign function and keeps the call behind unsafe.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

foreign "c" let puts (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt;

export let announce (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt :=
  unsafe { puts(message); };

foreign "c" names the native calling world. unsafe { ... } marks the operation that needs extra care.

Pointer helpers

Use @std/ffi pointer helpers when the native boundary gives you pointer-shaped values.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

let pointer := ffi.ptr.null[Int]();
let samePointer := unsafe { ffi.ptr.offset[Int](pointer, 0); };
ffi.ptr.isNull[Int](samePointer);

Keep raw native work small. Convert it to ordinary Musi data before passing values deeper into the program.
summaryHtml: Keep native calls explicit with foreign declarations, @std/ffi types, and unsafe blocks.

title: Overview
description: Translate Go 1.26.2 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
summary: Start from Go 1.26.2 habits, then read equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go 1.26.2 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
html:
Musi for Go Developers

Go 1.26.2 is the comparison point for this guide. Go developers often bring short declarations, explicit errors, slices, maps, structs, interfaces, goroutines, channels, context cancellation, packages, tests, unsafe, and cgo boundaries. Musi keeps those jobs recognizable while making values, data shape, and outside work explicit.

Go usually starts with a small function and a call:

func total(basePrice int, fee int) int {
    return basePrice + fee
}

answer := total(1200, 45)

Musi gives the function the same inputs and result, then lets the body expression produce the value.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Reading path

Read the guide from everyday Go toward deeper translation points:

  1. values, functions, blocks, and mutation;
  2. structs, slices, maps, nil, results, and effects;
  3. goroutines, channels, context, interfaces, generics, and receiver calls;
  4. packages, tests, unsafe, cgo, and FFI boundaries.
summaryHtml: Start from Go 1.26.2 habits, then read equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.

title: Values, Functions, and Expressions
description: Translate Go functions and expression habits into Musi expression-bodied functions and named calls.
summary: Use expression bodies, positional calls, and named calls for readable functions.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go functions and expression habits into Musi expression-bodied functions and named calls.
html:
Values, Functions, and Expressions

Go functions name parameters and return types, then use return to produce the result:

func total(basePrice int, fee int) int {
    return basePrice + fee
}

answer := total(1200, 45)

Musi keeps the signature explicit. The function body is the result expression.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Named calls

Go call sites are positional:

selected := render(8080, true)

That works, but booleans and repeated numbers become harder to read. Musi lets the call site name arguments when labels carry meaning.

let render (port : Int, secure : Bool) : Int := port;

let selected := render(port := 8080, secure := 0 = 0);
selected;
summaryHtml: Use expression bodies, positional calls, and named calls for readable functions.

title: Blocks, Branching, and Loops
description: Translate Go block statements, switches, and loops into Musi block expressions, match, recursion, and pipelines.
summary: Use blocks as values and model repetition with recursion or library traversal.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go block statements, switches, and loops into Musi block expressions, match, recursion, and pipelines.
html:
Blocks, Branching, and Loops

Go blocks group statements and return explicitly when a value leaves a function:

func invoiceTotal() int {
    basePrice := 1200
    fee := 45
    return basePrice + fee
}

Musi blocks can produce the final value directly.

let invoiceTotal () : Int :=
  (
    let basePrice := 1200;
    let fee := 45;
    basePrice + fee
  );

invoiceTotal();

Go loops commonly mutate loop state:

func totalSeats(groups int) int {
    seats := 0
    for remaining := groups; remaining > 0; remaining -= 1 {
        seats += 4
    }
    return seats
}

Musi uses recursion when the loop is a small state machine.

let rec totalSeats (remaining : Int, seats : Int) : Int :=
  match remaining (
  | 0 => seats
  | _ => totalSeats(remaining - 1, seats + 4)
  );

totalSeats(3, 0);

For collections, prefer library traversal and pipelines. For changing counters or accumulators, make the state explicit in recursive parameters.
summaryHtml: Use blocks as values and model repetition with recursion or library traversal.

title: Variables, Short Declarations, and Mutation
description: Translate Go `:=` and reassignment into explicit Musi bindings and mutation.
summary: Use mutable bindings for real changing state and fresh names for calculation stages.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go := and reassignment into explicit Musi bindings and mutation.
html:
Variables, Short Declarations, and Mutation

Go uses := for new locals and = for reassignment:

visits := 0
visits = visits + 1

nextVisits := visits + 1

Musi marks the binding that can change.

let visits := mut 0;
visits := visits + 1;

let nextVisits := visits + 1;
nextVisits;

Fresh values

Go often uses new names for calculation stages:

basePrice := 1200
total := basePrice + 45

Musi bindings are stable unless marked mutable, so fresh names fit ordinary calculations.

let basePrice := 1200;
let total := basePrice + 45;
total;
summaryHtml: Use mutable bindings for real changing state and fresh names for calculation stages.

title: Structs, Records, and Field Updates
description: Translate Go structs and struct literals into Musi records and field updates.
summary: Keep field names visible when constructing and updating data.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go structs and struct literals into Musi records and field updates.
html:
Structs, Records, and Field Updates

Go structs make compact named data:

type Endpoint struct {
    Host   string
    Port   int
    Secure bool
}

local := Endpoint{Host: "localhost", Port: 8080, Secure: false}
publicEndpoint := local
publicEndpoint.Host = "api.example.com"
publicEndpoint.Secure = true

Musi record-style data keeps field names visible at construction and update sites.

let Endpoint := data {
  host : String;
  port : Int;
  secure : Bool;
};

let local := {
  host := "localhost",
  port := 8080,
  secure := 0 = 1
};

let publicEndpoint := { ...local, host := "api.example.com", secure := 0 = 0 };
publicEndpoint.port;

Use record updates when the new value is mostly the old value with a few changed fields.
summaryHtml: Keep field names visible when constructing and updating data.

title: Slices, Arrays, Maps, and Pipelines
description: Translate Go slices and map-style transformations into Musi collection pipelines.
summary: Use pipelines when collection work should read left to right.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go slices and map-style transformations into Musi collection pipelines.
html:
Slices, Arrays, Maps, and Pipelines

Go slices often grow with append:

ports := []int{3000, 8080}
ports = append(ports, 9000)
visible := append([]int{}, ports...)

Musi pipelines keep the collection moving left to right.

let iter := import "@std/iter";

let ports := [3000, 8080];
let visible := ports
  |> iter.append[Int](9000)
  |> iter.collect[Int]();
visible;

Go maps are good for keyed lookup:

ports := map[string]int{"web": 8080, "admin": 9000}
port := ports["web"]

When absence matters, Musi examples should return Option instead of relying on a zero value.

let option := import "@std/option";

let lookupPort (name : String) : option.Option[Int] :=
  match name (
  | "admin" => option.some[Int](9000)
  | "web" => option.some[Int](8080)
  | _ => option.none[Int]()
  );

lookupPort("web")
  |> option.unwrapOr[Int](3000);
summaryHtml: Use pipelines when collection work should read left to right.

title: Nil, Option, and Result
description: Translate Go nil checks and comma-ok patterns into Musi Option and Result values.
summary: Use Option for absence and Result for failure that carries information.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go nil checks and comma-ok patterns into Musi Option and Result values.
html:
Nil, Option, and Result

Go has nil for pointers, slices, maps, channels, functions, and interfaces. Map lookup also has a comma-ok form:

ports := map[string]int{"admin": 9000}
port, ok := ports["web"]
if !ok {
    port = 8080
}

Musi uses the stdlib Option shape when a value may be absent.

let option := import "@std/option";

let lookupPort (name : String) : option.Option[Int] :=
  match name (
  | "admin" => option.some[Int](9000)
  | _ => option.none[Int]()
  );

let port := lookupPort("web")
  |> option.unwrapOr[Int](8080);
port;

Failure with information

Go commonly returns (value, error):

func parsePort(text string) (int, error) {
    if text == "8080" {
        return 8080, nil
    }
    return 0, errors.New("invalid port")
}

Musi uses Result when callers should receive either a value or an error.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("invalid port")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;
summaryHtml: Use Option for absence and Result for failure that carries information.

title: Errors, Results, and Effects
description: Translate Go explicit error returns and host interaction into Musi Result values and effect boundaries.
summary: Keep expected failure as data and outside work behind effectful APIs.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go explicit error returns and host interaction into Musi Result values and effect boundaries.
html:
Errors, Results, and Effects

Go makes expected failure explicit with error:

func parsePort(text string) (int, error) {
    if text == "8080" {
        return 8080, nil
    }
    return 0, errors.New("parse error")
}

port, err := parsePort("abc")
if err != nil {
    port = 3000
}

Musi keeps expected failure in the returned value.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("parse error")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;

Outside work

Go standard input and output use packages directly:

reader := bufio.NewReader(os.Stdin)
name, _ := reader.ReadString('\n')
fmt.Println(strings.TrimSpace(name))

Musi keeps console work at effectful boundaries.

let io := import "@std/io";

let name := io.promptTrimmed("name> ");
io.writeLine(name);
summaryHtml: Keep expected failure as data and outside work behind effectful APIs.

title: Goroutines, Channels, Context, and Effects
description: Translate Go concurrency habits into Musi effect boundaries and handlers.
summary: Use effects to name outside work and handler boundaries to decide how it runs.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go concurrency habits into Musi effect boundaries and handlers.
html:
Goroutines, Channels, Context, and Effects

Go concurrency often starts a goroutine, sends through a channel, and waits for a value:

func loadPort() int {
    done := make(chan int, 1)
    go func() {
        done <- 8080
    }()
    return <-done
}

Musi does not hide outside work behind a goroutine marker in ordinary expressions. Name the operation as an effect request, then decide how it is interpreted at the boundary.

let PortSource := effect {
  let loadPort () : Int;
};

let loadPort () using { PortSource } : Int :=
  request PortSource.loadPort();

Go context.Context carries cancellation and deadlines through call chains:

func readPort(ctx context.Context) (int, error) {
    select {
    case <-ctx.Done():
        return 0, ctx.Err()
    default:
        return 8080, nil
    }
}

In Musi, pass ordinary data as data and keep cancellation, scheduling, or host interaction in the effect surface that the handler owns.

let PortSource := effect {
  let loadPort () : Int;
};

handle PortSource.loadPort() using PortSource {
  value => value;
  loadPort(k) => resume 8080;
};
summaryHtml: Use effects to name outside work and handler boundaries to decide how it runs.

title: Interfaces, Classes, Instances, and Laws
description: Translate Go interfaces into Musi classes, instances, and laws.
summary: Use classes for required behavior and laws for behavior expectations.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go interfaces into Musi classes, instances, and laws.
html:
Interfaces, Classes, Instances, and Laws

Go interfaces name behavior structurally:

type Vehicle interface {
    Wheels() int
}

type Car struct{}

func (Car) Wheels() int {
    return 4
}

Musi classes state required operations. Instances attach those operations to a type.

let Vehicle[T] := class {
  let wheels(self : T) : Int;
  law atLeastFourWheels(vehicle : T) := vehicle.wheels() >= 4;
};

let Car := data {
  | Car
};

let carVehicle := instance Vehicle[Car] {
  let wheels(self : Car) : Int := 4;
};

A real-world analogy: a car is a vehicle, but road rules require at least four wheels for this category. The class names the operation. The law records the expectation implementations should satisfy.
summaryHtml: Use classes for required behavior and laws for behavior expectations.

title: Generics and Type Constructors
description: Translate Go generic functions and generic data into Musi type parameters and type constructors.
summary: Use bracketed type parameters for reusable functions, data, and higher-kinded constraints.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go generic functions and generic data into Musi type parameters and type constructors.
html:
Generics and Type Constructors

Go generics put type parameters after the function name:

func Identity[T any](input T) T {
    return input
}

port := Identity[int](8080)

Musi also uses bracketed type parameters for reusable functions.

let identity[T] (input : T) : T := input;

let port := identity[Int](8080);
port;

Generic data follows the same pattern.

type Box[T any] struct {
    Value T
}

box := Box[int]{Value: 8080}

Musi can pass a generic data constructor where a class expects a type constructor.

let Box[T] := data {
  | Box(value : T)
};

let Keeps[F : Type -> Type] := class {
  let keep(value : F[Int]) : F[Int];
};

let boxKeeps := instance Keeps[Box] {
  let keep(value : Box[Int]) : Box[Int] := value;
};

let box := .Box(value := 8080);
boxKeeps.keep(box);
summaryHtml: Use bracketed type parameters for reusable functions, data, and higher-kinded constraints.

title: Methods and Receiver Calls
description: Translate Go methods into Musi receiver-style calls.
summary: Use receiver calls when the operation reads naturally from the left-hand value.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go methods into Musi receiver-style calls.
html:
Methods and Receiver Calls

Go methods place the receiver before the function name:

type Port int

func (port Port) WithOffset(offset int) Port {
    return port + Port(offset)
}

publicPort := Port(8080).WithOffset(1)

Musi receiver methods keep the transformed value on the left at the call site.

let (port : Int).withOffset (offset : Int) : Int := port + offset;

let publicPort := 8080.withOffset(1);
publicPort;

Use ordinary functions when several inputs are equally important. Use receiver calls when the left-hand value is the thing being transformed.
summaryHtml: Use receiver calls when the operation reads naturally from the left-hand value.

title: Packages, Imports, and Exports
description: Translate Go packages and exported names into Musi imports and exports.
summary: Use import and export directly to describe package-facing boundaries.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go packages and exported names into Musi imports and exports.
html:
Packages, Imports, and Exports

Go exposes package members by capitalizing exported names:

package ports

func DefaultPort() int {
    return 8080
}

Musi exports the package-facing value directly.

export let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

Go imports a package and calls through its package name:

import "example.com/app/ports"

port := ports.DefaultPort()

Musi imports a module value and calls through it.

let Ports := import "./ports";

let port := Ports.defaultPort();
port;
summaryHtml: Use import and export directly to describe package-facing boundaries.

title: Testing and Tooling
description: Translate Go test habits into small Musi package tests and toolchain checks.
summary: Write tests as small examples and use the package toolchain for routine checks.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go test habits into small Musi package tests and toolchain checks.
html:
Testing and Tooling

Go tests name one behavior and use testing.T for failure reporting:

func TestDefaultPort(t *testing.T) {
    if DefaultPort() != 8080 {
        t.Fatalf("default port mismatch")
    }
}

Musi tests keep the rule name, the small value, and the expectation together.

let testing := import "@std/testing";

testing.describe("ports");
testing.it("default port", testing.equal[Int](8080, 8080));
testing.endDescribe();

Use the package toolchain for formatting, checking, and running tests so local work matches CI behavior.
summaryHtml: Write tests as small examples and use the package toolchain for routine checks.

title: Unsafe, Cgo, and FFI
description: Translate Go unsafe and cgo boundaries into Musi foreign declarations, FFI types, pointer helpers, and unsafe blocks.
summary: Keep ABI calls explicit and convert native shapes into ordinary Musi values near the boundary.
descriptionHtml: Translate Go unsafe and cgo boundaries into Musi foreign declarations, FFI types, pointer helpers, and unsafe blocks.
html:
Unsafe, Cgo, and FFI

Go cgo and unsafe make native boundaries explicit:

/*
#include <stdio.h>
*/
import "C"

func announce(message *C.char) C.int {
    return C.puts(message)
}

Musi declares the C ABI boundary with foreign "c" and calls it in an unsafe block.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

foreign "c" let puts (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt;

export let announce (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt :=
  unsafe { puts(message); };

Pointer helpers

Go ordinary code avoids pointer arithmetic. Native libraries and unsafe.Pointer can still cross raw memory boundaries.

var pointer unsafe.Pointer
isNull := pointer == nil

Musi keeps raw pointer work in @std/ffi helpers.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

let pointer := ffi.ptr.null[Int]();
let samePointer := unsafe { ffi.ptr.offset[Int](pointer, 0); };
ffi.ptr.isNull[Int](samePointer);
summaryHtml: Keep ABI calls explicit and convert native shapes into ordinary Musi values near the boundary.

title: Overview
description: Translate Java 17 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
summary: Start from Java 17 habits, then read equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java 17 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
html:
Musi for Java Developers

Java 17 is the comparison point for this guide. Java developers often bring classes, records, sealed interfaces, exceptions, streams, packages, modules, annotations, and JNI boundaries. Musi keeps those jobs recognizable while making values, effects, and data shape explicit.

Java usually puts small calculations inside methods:

static int total(int basePrice, int fee) {
    return basePrice + fee;
}

Musi keeps the same input and output types, but the body is the produced value.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Reading path

The guide moves from everyday Java code toward deeper language design:

  1. values, methods, and expressions;
  2. blocks, control flow, variables, and mutation;
  3. records, sealed types, collections, null, results, and effects;
  4. generics, interfaces, packages, tests, native boundaries, and unsafe code.
summaryHtml: Start from Java 17 habits, then read equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.

title: Values, Methods, and Expressions
description: Translate Java methods and expression habits into Musi expression-bodied functions and named calls.
summary: Use expression bodies, positional calls, and named calls for readable functions.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java methods and expression habits into Musi expression-bodied functions and named calls.
html:
Values, Methods, and Expressions

Java methods usually wrap work in a statement body:

static int total(int basePrice, int fee) {
    return basePrice + fee;
}

int answer = total(1200, 45);

Musi functions name parameters and output type directly, and the body expression is the result.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Named calls

Java call sites are positional:

int selected = render(8080, true);

That is compact, but numbers and booleans can become hard to read. Musi supports named calls when labels help.

let render (port : Int, secure : Bool) : Int := port;

let selected := render(port := 8080, secure := 0 = 0);
selected;
summaryHtml: Use expression bodies, positional calls, and named calls for readable functions.

title: Blocks and Control Flow
description: Translate Java statement blocks and loops into Musi block expressions, match, recursion, and pipelines.
summary: Use blocks as values and model repetition with recursion or library traversal.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java statement blocks and loops into Musi block expressions, match, recursion, and pipelines.
html:
Blocks and Control Flow

Java blocks group statements and usually require return for produced values:

static int invoiceTotal() {
    int basePrice = 1200;
    int fee = 45;
    return basePrice + fee;
}

Musi blocks can produce the final value directly.

let invoiceTotal () : Int :=
  (
    let basePrice := 1200;
    let fee := 45;
    basePrice + fee
  );

invoiceTotal();

Repetition

Java loop state is commonly mutated:

static int totalSeats(int groups) {
    int seats = 0;
    for (int remaining = groups; remaining > 0; remaining -= 1) {
        seats += 4;
    }
    return seats;
}

Musi can make the changing state visible through recursive parameters.

let rec totalSeats (remaining : Int, seats : Int) : Int :=
  match remaining (
  | 0 => seats
  | _ => totalSeats(remaining - 1, seats + 4)
  );

totalSeats(3, 0);

Use library traversal and pipelines for collection work. Use let rec when the loop is really a small state machine.
summaryHtml: Use blocks as values and model repetition with recursion or library traversal.

title: Variables and Mutation
description: Translate Java reassignment into explicit Musi mutation and fresh values.
summary: Use mutable bindings for real changing state and fresh names for calculation stages.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java reassignment into explicit Musi mutation and fresh values.
html:
Variables and Mutation

Java local variables can be reassigned unless they are final:

int visits = 0;
visits = visits + 1;

int nextVisits = visits + 1;

Musi marks the binding that can change.

let visits := mut 0;
visits := visits + 1;

let nextVisits := visits + 1;
nextVisits;

Fresh values

Java developers often use final to make calculation stages stable:

final int basePrice = 1200;
final int total = basePrice + 45;

Musi bindings are stable by default, so fresh names fit ordinary calculations.

let basePrice := 1200;
let total := basePrice + 45;
total;
summaryHtml: Use mutable bindings for real changing state and fresh names for calculation stages.

title: Records, Classes, and Objects
description: Translate Java records and classes into Musi records, variants, methods, and behavior contracts.
summary: Separate data shape from behavior so construction and updates stay visible.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java records and classes into Musi records, variants, methods, and behavior contracts.
html:
Records, Classes, and Objects

Java 17 records make small immutable data carriers concise:

record Endpoint(String host, int port, boolean secure) {}

var local = new Endpoint("localhost", 8080, false);
var publicEndpoint = new Endpoint("api.example.com", local.port(), true);

Musi record-style data keeps the field names visible at construction and update sites.

let Endpoint := data {
  host : String;
  port : Int;
  secure : Bool;
};

let local := {
  host := "localhost",
  port := 8080,
  secure := 0 = 1
};

let publicEndpoint := { ...local, host := "api.example.com", secure := 0 = 0 };
publicEndpoint.port;

Java classes combine state and behavior. Musi separates the data shape from functions, methods, classes, and instances so each part stays visible.
summaryHtml: Separate data shape from behavior so construction and updates stay visible.

title: Collections, Streams, and Pipelines
description: Translate Java collections and streams into Musi collection pipelines.
summary: Use pipelines for collection transformations that should read left to right.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java collections and streams into Musi collection pipelines.
html:
Collections, Streams, and Pipelines

Java collections often combine a collection type with mutation or streams:

var ports = new ArrayList<Integer>(List.of(3000, 8080));
ports.add(9000);
var visible = List.copyOf(ports);

Streams make transformations read more declaratively:

var visible = Stream.concat(Stream.of(3000, 8080), Stream.of(9000))
    .toList();

Musi pipelines keep the value moving left to right.

let iter := import "@std/iter";

let ports := [3000, 8080];
let visible := ports
  |> iter.append[Int](9000)
  |> iter.collect[Int]();
visible;
summaryHtml: Use pipelines for collection transformations that should read left to right.

title: Null, Option, and Result
description: Translate Java null, Optional, and expected failure into Musi Option and Result values.
summary: Use Option for absence and Result for failure that carries information.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java null, Optional, and expected failure into Musi Option and Result values.
html:
Null, Option, and Result

Java has null, and Java 17 also has Optional for maybe-present values:

static Optional<Integer> lookupPort(String name) {
    if (name.equals("admin")) {
        return Optional.of(9000);
    }
    return Optional.empty();
}

int port = lookupPort("web").orElse(8080);

Musi uses the stdlib Option shape for ordinary absence.

let option := import "@std/option";

let lookupPort (name : String) : option.Option[Int] :=
  match name (
  | "admin" => option.some[Int](9000)
  | _ => option.none[Int]()
  );

let port := lookupPort("web")
  |> option.unwrapOr[Int](8080);
port;

Failure with information

Java often uses exceptions or small result classes when failure should carry information:

record ParsePort(boolean ok, int port, String message) {}

static ParsePort parsePort(String text) {
    if (text.equals("8080")) {
        return new ParsePort(true, 8080, "");
    }
    return new ParsePort(false, 0, "invalid port");
}

Musi uses Result when callers should receive either a value or an error.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("invalid port")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;
summaryHtml: Use Option for absence and Result for failure that carries information.

title: Exceptions and Effects
description: Translate Java exceptions and host interaction into Musi Result values and effect boundaries.
summary: Keep expected failure as data and outside work behind effectful APIs.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java exceptions and host interaction into Musi Result values and effect boundaries.
html:
Exceptions and Effects

Java exceptions move failure through control flow:

static int parsePort(String text) {
    if (text.equals("8080")) {
        return 8080;
    }
    throw new IllegalArgumentException("parse error");
}

Musi keeps expected failure in the returned value.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("parse error")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;

Outside work

Java host interaction usually calls library APIs directly:

var scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
String name = scanner.nextLine().trim();
System.out.println(name);

Musi keeps console work at effectful boundaries.

let io := import "@std/io";

let name := io.promptTrimmed("name> ");
io.writeLine(name);
summaryHtml: Keep expected failure as data and outside work behind effectful APIs.

title: Sealed Types and Patterns
description: Translate Java sealed interfaces and pattern matching into Musi data variants and match patterns.
summary: Model alternatives with payload-carrying variants and inspect them with match.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java sealed interfaces and pattern matching into Musi data variants and match patterns.
html:
Sealed Types and Patterns

Java 17 sealed interfaces can model a closed set of alternatives:

sealed interface TaskState permits Waiting, Running, Done {}
record Waiting() implements TaskState {}
record Running(int id) implements TaskState {}
record Done(int code) implements TaskState {}

TaskState state = new Running(42);

Musi variants put the closed alternatives and payloads in one data definition.

let TaskState := data {
  | Waiting
  | Running(id : Int)
  | Done(code : Int)
};

let state : TaskState := .Running(id := 42);
match state (
| .Running(id) => id
| .Waiting => 0
| .Done(code) => code
);

match makes each case visible at the use site.
summaryHtml: Model alternatives with payload-carrying variants and inspect them with match.

title: Generics, Interfaces, and Laws
description: Translate Java generics and interfaces into Musi type parameters, classes, instances, and laws.
summary: Use generic functions for reuse and classes with laws for behavior contracts.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java generics and interfaces into Musi type parameters, classes, instances, and laws.
html:
Generics, Interfaces, and Laws

Java generics make reusable functions and types explicit:

static <T> T identity(T input) {
    return input;
}

int port = identity(8080);

Musi uses type parameters for the same reusable shape.

let identity[T] (input : T) : T := input;

let port := identity[Int](8080);
port;

Interfaces and laws

Java interfaces describe required behavior:

interface Vehicle {
    int wheels();
}

record Car() implements Vehicle {
    public int wheels() {
        return 4;
    }
}

Musi classes state required operations, and laws record behavior expectations that implementations should satisfy.

let Vehicle[T] := class {
  let wheels(self : T) : Int;
  law atLeastFourWheels(vehicle : T) := vehicle.wheels() >= 4;
};

let Car := data {
  | Car
};

let carVehicle := instance Vehicle[Car] {
  let wheels(self : Car) : Int := 4;
};
summaryHtml: Use generic functions for reuse and classes with laws for behavior contracts.

title: Methods and Receiver Calls
description: Translate Java instance methods into Musi receiver-style calls.
summary: Use receiver calls when the operation reads naturally from the left-hand value.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java instance methods into Musi receiver-style calls.
html:
Methods and Receiver Calls

Java puts the receiver before the dot:

record Port(int value) {
    int withOffset(int offset) {
        return value + offset;
    }
}

int publicPort = new Port(8080).withOffset(1);

Musi can use receiver-style calls for operations that read naturally from the left-hand value.

let (port : Int).withOffset (offset : Int) : Int := port + offset;

let publicPort := 8080.withOffset(1);
publicPort;

Use ordinary functions when several inputs are equally important. Use receiver calls when the left-hand value is the thing being transformed.
summaryHtml: Use receiver calls when the operation reads naturally from the left-hand value.

title: Packages and Modules
description: Translate Java packages and modules into Musi imports, exports, and package boundaries.
summary: Use import and export directly to describe package-facing boundaries.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java packages and modules into Musi imports, exports, and package boundaries.
html:
Packages and Modules

Java package-facing code often starts with a package declaration and public type:

package ports;

public final class Ports {
    public static int defaultPort() {
        return 8080;
    }
}

Musi exports the package-facing value directly.

export let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

Java imports another package member by name:

import ports.Ports;

int port = Ports.defaultPort();

Musi imports a module value and calls through it.

let Ports := import "./ports";

let port := Ports.defaultPort();
port;
summaryHtml: Use import and export directly to describe package-facing boundaries.

title: Testing and Tooling
description: Translate Java test habits into small Musi package tests and toolchain checks.
summary: Write tests as small examples and use the package toolchain for routine checks.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java test habits into small Musi package tests and toolchain checks.
html:
Testing and Tooling

Java tests often name one rule and assert the expected value:

@Test
void defaultPortIs8080() {
    assertEquals(8080, Ports.defaultPort());
}

Musi tests keep the rule name, the small value, and the expectation together.

let testing := import "@std/testing";

testing.describe("ports");
testing.it("default port", testing.equal[Int](8080, 8080));
testing.endDescribe();

Use the package toolchain for formatting, checking, and running tests so local work matches CI behavior.
summaryHtml: Write tests as small examples and use the package toolchain for routine checks.

title: Native, Unsafe, and FFI
description: Translate Java native boundaries into Musi foreign declarations, FFI types, pointer helpers, and unsafe blocks.
summary: Keep ABI calls explicit and convert native shapes into ordinary Musi values near the boundary.
descriptionHtml: Translate Java native boundaries into Musi foreign declarations, FFI types, pointer helpers, and unsafe blocks.
html:
Native, Unsafe, and FFI

Java native boundaries use native declarations and host runtime support:

final class NativeConsole {
    static native int puts(String message);
}

Musi declares the C ABI boundary with foreign "c" and calls it in an unsafe block.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

foreign "c" let puts (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt;

export let announce (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt :=
  unsafe { puts(message); };

Pointer helpers

Java ordinary code does not expose raw pointer arithmetic. JNI and native libraries can still produce raw memory boundaries.

long pointer = nativePointer();
boolean isNull = pointer == 0L;

Musi keeps raw pointer work in @std/ffi helpers.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

let pointer := ffi.ptr.null[Int]();
let samePointer := unsafe { ffi.ptr.offset[Int](pointer, 0); };
ffi.ptr.isNull[Int](samePointer);
summaryHtml: Keep ABI calls explicit and convert native shapes into ordinary Musi values near the boundary.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Overview
description: Translate ES6 JavaScript and TypeScript 5.9 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
summary: Start from ES6 JavaScript runtime habits and TypeScript 5.9 type habits, then read the equivalent Musi shapes.
descriptionHtml: Translate ES6 JavaScript and TypeScript 5.9 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
html:
ES6 JavaScript supplies the runtime habits for this guide: objects, functions, arrays, promises, modules, and tests. TypeScript 5.9 is the typed comparison point for annotations, generic functions, union modeling, and import habits.

Each page starts with JavaScript or TypeScript code, then shows the matching Musi code with the same names and the same job.

First translation

TypeScript has several function forms. The ordinary declaration reads like this:

function total(base: number, fee: number): number {
  return base + fee;
}

const answer = total(1200, 45);
answer;

The closer shape to Musi is a named value that stores a function:

const total = function (base: number, fee: number): number {
  return base + fee;
};

const answer = total(1200, 45);
answer;

Musi keeps the named value shape, but the function body is an expression. The final expression is the result.

let total (base : Int, fee : Int) : Int := base + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Reading path

  • Values, Functions, and Final Expressions
  • let, const, and Mutable State
  • Objects, Records, and Field Updates
  • Arrays and Data Pipelines
  • Null, Undefined, Option, and Result
  • Unions, Variants, and Pattern Matching
  • Generics and Type Parameters
  • Promises, Async, and Effects
  • Modules, Packages, and Imports
  • Classes, Objects, and Shared Behavior
  • Testing and Tooling

Habits that transfer

  • keep small functions
  • name object fields clearly
  • make module boundaries readable
  • use type annotations where readers need certainty
  • keep side effects at visible edges

Habits to translate

  • return becomes the final expression
  • object spread becomes record spread
  • null and undefined become explicit data shapes
  • TypeScript unions become data variants when cases carry meaning
  • promises and async work become effect requests at boundaries
summaryHtml: Start from ES6 JavaScript runtime habits and TypeScript 5.9 type habits, then read the equivalent Musi shapes.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Values, Functions, and Final Expressions
description: Translate JavaScript and TypeScript functions into Musi `let` functions.
summary: Use `let` for values and functions, and use final expressions instead of `return`.
descriptionHtml: Translate JavaScript and TypeScript functions into Musi let functions.
html:
JavaScript can declare a function directly:

function total(base, fee) {
  return base + fee;
}

const answer = total(1200, 45);
answer;

JavaScript can also bind a function value to a name. That shape is closer to Musi:

const total = function (base, fee) {
  return base + fee;
};

const answer = total(1200, 45);
answer;

TypeScript adds parameter and result types to either form:

const total = function (base: number, fee: number): number {
  return base + fee;
};

const answer = total(1200, 45);
answer;

Musi puts the function in a let binding and writes the result type after the parameters.

let total (base : Int, fee : Int) : Int := base + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

The last expression leaves the value. There is no return keyword in ordinary Musi function bodies.

Named calls

JavaScript and TypeScript often use an options object when a call has several values:

function render(input: { port: number; secure: boolean }): number {
  return input.port;
}

const selected = render({ port: 8080, secure: true });
selected;

Musi can name arguments at the call site without wrapping them in an object.

let render (port : Int, secure : Bool) : Int := port;

let selected := render(port := 8080, secure := 0 = 0);
selected;
summaryHtml: Use let for values and functions, and use final expressions instead of return.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: `let`, `const`, and Mutable State
description: Translate JavaScript and TypeScript binding habits into Musi value mutation.
summary: `const`-style names stay ordinary; changing values use explicit mutable values.
descriptionHtml: Translate JavaScript and TypeScript binding habits into Musi value mutation.
html:
JavaScript separates rebinding from fixed binding with let and const:

let visits = 0;
visits = visits + 1;
visits;

Musi keeps the name ordinary and puts mutability on the value.

let visits := mut 0;
visits := visits + 1;
visits;

Use mutation when the same local value changes over time. Use a new let when a step produces a new value.

const base = 1200;
const total = base + 45;
total;
let base := 1200;
let total := base + 45;
total;
summaryHtml: const-style names stay ordinary; changing values use explicit mutable values.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Objects, Records, and Field Updates
description: Translate object literals, structural types, and object spread into Musi records.
summary: Object literals map to record values, and object spread maps to record spread updates.
descriptionHtml: Translate object literals, structural types, and object spread into Musi records.
html:
JavaScript object literals carry named fields:

const local = {
  host: "localhost",
  port: 8080,
  secure: false,
};

const secure = { ...local, secure: true };
secure.port;

TypeScript usually gives that object a named shape:

type Endpoint = {
  host: string;
  port: number;
  secure: boolean;
};

Musi can name the record-shaped data and use record spread for updates.

let Endpoint := data {
  host : String;
  port : Int;
  secure : Bool;
};

let local := {
  host := "localhost",
  port := 8080,
  secure := 0 = 1
};

let secure := { ...local, secure := 0 = 0 };
secure.port;

Read { ...local, secure := 0 = 0 } as "copy the fields from local, then replace secure."
summaryHtml: Object literals map to record values, and object spread maps to record spread updates.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Arrays and Data Pipelines
description: Translate JavaScript array helper habits into Musi pipeline-friendly code.
summary: Arrays stay data; pipeline calls keep transformations readable from left to right.
descriptionHtml: Translate JavaScript array helper habits into Musi pipeline-friendly code.
html:
JavaScript often chains array helpers:

const ports = [3000, 8080, 9000];
const visible = ports.map((port) => port + 1);
visible;

Musi keeps the array value visible and can pipe it through library functions.

let iter := import "@std/iter";

let ports := [3000, 8080, 9000];
let visible := ports
  |> iter.map[Int, Int](\(port : Int) : Int => port + 1);
visible;

Use a pipeline when the output of one step becomes the input to the next. Use a named let when the step needs a domain name.
summaryHtml: Arrays stay data; pipeline calls keep transformations readable from left to right.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Null, Undefined, Option, and Result
description: Translate nullable JavaScript and TypeScript values into stdlib Option and Result values.
summary: `null` and `undefined` habits become `@std/option` values when absence matters.
descriptionHtml: Translate nullable JavaScript and TypeScript values into stdlib Option and Result values.
html:
JavaScript commonly uses null when a value is missing:

function findPort(name) {
  return name === "local" ? 8080 : null;
}

const port = findPort("local");
port;

TypeScript makes that absence visible in the type:

function findPort(name: string): number | null {
  return name === "local" ? 8080 : null;
}

Musi uses @std/option for ordinary absence. The type names the possibility of no value, and the helper chooses the fallback.

let option := import "@std/option";

let findPort (name : String) : option.Option[Int] := option.some[Int](8080);

let port := findPort("local")
  |> option.unwrapOr[Int](3000);
port;

For recoverable failure with a reason, use @std/result instead of making another result-shaped type.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] := result.ok[Int, String](8080);

let port := parsePort("8080")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;
summaryHtml: null and undefined habits become @std/option values when absence matters.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Unions, Variants, and Pattern Matching
description: Translate TypeScript discriminated unions into Musi data variants and `match`.
summary: Discriminated union cases map to constructor-style `data` variants and `match` arms.
descriptionHtml: Translate TypeScript discriminated unions into Musi data variants and match.
html:
TypeScript discriminated unions give each case a tag:

type LoadState =
  | { kind: "loading" }
  | { kind: "loaded"; value: number }
  | { kind: "failed"; message: string };

const state: LoadState = { kind: "loaded", value: 8080 };

const port =
  state.kind === "loaded" ? state.value : 3000;

Musi uses variants instead of string tags.

let LoadState := data {
  | Loading
  | Loaded(value : Int)
  | Failed(message : String)
};

let state : LoadState := .Loaded(value := 8080);
match state (
| .Loaded\(value) => value
| .Loading => 3000
| .Failed(message) => 3000
);

match names every shape the code expects. Payload names live with the constructor and come back in the matching arm.
summaryHtml: Discriminated union cases map to constructor-style data variants and match arms.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Generics and Type Parameters
description: Translate TypeScript generic functions and generic objects into Musi type parameters.
summary: TypeScript type parameters map to Musi bracketed type parameters.
