descriptionHtml: Translate TypeScript generic functions and generic objects into Musi type parameters.
html:
TypeScript generic functions keep one implementation for many input types:

function identity<T>(input: T): T {
  return input;
}

const port = identity<number>(8080);
port;

Musi uses bracketed type parameters on the function name.

let identity[T] (input : T) : T := input;

let port := identity[Int](8080);
port;

Generic data uses the same bracketed type parameter shape.

type Box<T> = {
  value: T;
};

const boxed: Box<number> = { value: 8080 };
boxed.value;
let Box1[T] := data {
  | Box1(value : T)
};

let boxed := .Box1(value := 8080);
match boxed (
| .Box1\(value) => value
);
summaryHtml: TypeScript type parameters map to Musi bracketed type parameters.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Promises, Async, and I/O Boundaries
description: Translate JavaScript promises and TypeScript async boundaries into Musi stdlib I/O.
summary: Boundary work that JavaScript models with promises usually starts at `@std/io` in Musi.
descriptionHtml: Translate JavaScript promises and TypeScript async boundaries into Musi stdlib I/O.
html:
JavaScript uses promises for input that arrives from outside the current stack:

async function readName(consoleApi) {
  const name = await consoleApi.readLine("name> ");
  console.log(name.trim());
}

TypeScript adds the promised result type:

async function readName(consoleApi: ConsoleApi): Promise<void> {
  const name: string = await consoleApi.readLine("name> ");
  console.log(name.trim());
}

Musi code normally reaches for the standard library wrapper. @std/io sits above the runtime request and gives user code stable names.

let io := import "@std/io";

let name := io.promptTrimmed("name> ");
io.writeLine(name);

Use stdlib modules when they exist. Define custom effects when you are designing a capability that the standard library does not already name.
summaryHtml: Boundary work that JavaScript models with promises usually starts at @std/io in Musi.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Modules, Packages, and Imports
description: Translate JavaScript and TypeScript module imports into Musi package imports.
summary: ES module imports map to Musi package imports and exported names.
descriptionHtml: Translate JavaScript and TypeScript module imports into Musi package imports.
html:
JavaScript modules export names:

export function defaultPort() {
  return 8080;
}

A consumer imports those names:

import { defaultPort } from "./ports.js";

const port = defaultPort();
port;

Musi exports names from a package file and imports that package as a value.

export let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;
let Ports := import "./ports";

let port := Ports.defaultPort();
port;
summaryHtml: ES module imports map to Musi package imports and exported names.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Classes, Objects, and Shared Behavior
description: Translate JavaScript classes and TypeScript interfaces into Musi classes and instances.
summary: Use Musi classes for shared behavior contracts, not as object constructors.
descriptionHtml: Translate JavaScript classes and TypeScript interfaces into Musi classes and instances.
html:
JavaScript classes often combine construction with behavior:

class Car {
  wheels() {
    return 4;
  }
}

const car = new Car();
car.wheels();

TypeScript can describe the behavior through an interface:

interface Vehicle {
  wheels(): number;
}

Musi separates data from shared behavior.

let Vehicle[T] := class {
  let wheels(self : T) : Int;
};

let Car := data {
  | Car
};

let carVehicle := instance Vehicle[Car] {
  let wheels(self : Car) : Int := 4;
};

Use data for the value shape. Use class and instance when several types need the same behavior.
summaryHtml: Use Musi classes for shared behavior contracts, not as object constructors.

sectionTitle: Musi for JavaScript and TypeScript Developers
title: Testing and Tooling
description: Translate JavaScript and TypeScript test habits into Musi package tests.
summary: Use `@std/testing` for small package tests that name behavior and return test results.
descriptionHtml: Translate JavaScript and TypeScript test habits into Musi package tests.
html:
JavaScript and TypeScript tests usually name behavior, call code, and assert a result:

function defaultPort(): number {
  return 8080;
}

test("default port is http alt", () => {
  expect(defaultPort()).toBe(8080);
});

Musi uses @std/testing for the same shape.

let testing := import "@std/testing";

let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

export let test () :=
  (
    testing.describe("ports");
    testing.it("default port is http alt", testing.toBe(defaultPort(), 8080));
    testing.endDescribe()
  );
summaryHtml: Use @std/testing for small package tests that name behavior and return test results.

title: Overview
description: Translate Lua 5.4.8 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
summary: Start from Lua 5.4.8 habits, then read equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua 5.4.8 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
html:
Musi for Lua Developers

Lua 5.4.8 is the comparison point for this guide. Lua developers often bring dynamic values, tables, metatables, closures, modules, nil, pcall, coroutines, embedding, and C API boundaries. Musi keeps those jobs recognizable while making values, data shape, and outside work explicit.

Lua usually starts with a small function and a local result:

local function total(basePrice, fee)
  return basePrice + fee
end

local answer = total(1200, 45)

Musi gives the function explicit input and output types. The body expression produces the result.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Reading path

Read the guide from everyday Lua toward deeper translation points:

  1. locals, functions, closures, blocks, and repetition;
  2. tables, sequences, maps, nil, results, and effects;
  3. coroutines, metatables, variants, modules, tests, and native boundaries.
summaryHtml: Start from Lua 5.4.8 habits, then read equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.

title: Values, Locals, and Expressions
description: Translate Lua locals and expression habits into Musi typed bindings and expression-bodied functions.
summary: Use typed function signatures and stable bindings for values that should stay clear.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua locals and expression habits into Musi typed bindings and expression-bodied functions.
html:
Values, Locals, and Expressions

Lua locals are dynamic and can hold any runtime value:

local function total(basePrice, fee)
  return basePrice + fee
end

local answer = total(1200, 45)

Musi keeps names local, but it records the input and output types at the function boundary.

let total (basePrice : Int, fee : Int) : Int := basePrice + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Lua code often relies on names and comments to explain argument meaning:

local selected = render(8080, true)

Musi supports named calls when labels make the call site clearer.

let render (port : Int, secure : Bool) : Int := port;

let selected := render(port := 8080, secure := 0 = 0);
selected;
summaryHtml: Use typed function signatures and stable bindings for values that should stay clear.

title: Functions, Closures, and Named Calls
description: Translate Lua function values and closures into Musi functions, lambdas, and named calls.
summary: Use functions for named behavior and lambdas for small callback-shaped values.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua function values and closures into Musi functions, lambdas, and named calls.
html:
Functions, Closures, and Named Calls

Lua treats functions as ordinary values:

local function makeAdder(step)
  return function(value)
    return value + step
  end
end

local addOne = makeAdder(1)
local nextPort = addOne(8080)

Musi lambdas use a leading backslash, so callback-shaped values stand out at the call site.

let makeAdder (step : Int) : Int -> Int :=
  \(value : Int) => value + step;

let addOne := makeAdder(1);
let nextPort := addOne(8080);
nextPort;

Use named calls when the meaning of a Lua positional argument would otherwise depend on memory.

let render (port : Int, secure : Bool) : Int := port;

let selected := render(port := 8080, secure := 0 = 0);
selected;
summaryHtml: Use functions for named behavior and lambdas for small callback-shaped values.

title: Blocks, Branching, and Repetition
description: Translate Lua blocks, branching, and loops into Musi block expressions, match, recursion, and pipelines.
summary: Use blocks as values and model repetition with recursion or library traversal.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua blocks, branching, and loops into Musi block expressions, match, recursion, and pipelines.
html:
Blocks, Branching, and Repetition

Lua blocks group statements and return when a function should produce a value:

local function invoiceTotal()
  local basePrice = 1200
  local fee = 45
  return basePrice + fee
end

Musi blocks can produce the final value directly.

let invoiceTotal () : Int :=
  (
    let basePrice := 1200;
    let fee := 45;
    basePrice + fee
  );

invoiceTotal();

Lua loops commonly mutate loop state:

local function totalSeats(groups)
  local seats = 0
  for _ = 1, groups do
    seats = seats + 4
  end
  return seats
end

Musi uses recursion when the loop is a small state machine.

let rec totalSeats (remaining : Int, seats : Int) : Int :=
  match remaining (
  | 0 => seats
  | _ => totalSeats(remaining - 1, seats + 4)
  );

totalSeats(3, 0);
summaryHtml: Use blocks as values and model repetition with recursion or library traversal.

title: Tables, Records, and Field Updates
description: Translate Lua table records into Musi records and field updates.
summary: Use records when table fields are known parts of the data shape.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua table records into Musi records and field updates.
html:
Tables, Records, and Field Updates

Lua tables often act as records:

local localEndpoint = { host = "localhost", port = 8080, secure = false }
local publicEndpoint = {
  host = "api.example.com",
  port = localEndpoint.port,
  secure = true,
}

Musi record-style data keeps the field names visible and typed.

let Endpoint := data {
  host : String;
  port : Int;
  secure : Bool;
};

let localEndpoint := {
  host := "localhost",
  port := 8080,
  secure := 0 = 1
};

let publicEndpoint := { ...localEndpoint, host := "api.example.com", secure := 0 = 0 };
publicEndpoint.port;

Use field updates when the new value is mostly the old value with a few changed fields.
summaryHtml: Use records when table fields are known parts of the data shape.

title: Arrays, Sequences, Maps, and Pipelines
description: Translate Lua sequence and table-map habits into Musi collection pipelines and Option values.
summary: Use pipelines for sequence work and Option when keyed lookup may be absent.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua sequence and table-map habits into Musi collection pipelines and Option values.
html:
Arrays, Sequences, Maps, and Pipelines

Lua sequences are tables with integer keys:

local ports = { 3000, 8080 }
table.insert(ports, 9000)

Musi pipelines keep the sequence moving left to right.

let iter := import "@std/iter";

let ports := [3000, 8080];
let visible := ports
  |> iter.append[Int](9000)
  |> iter.collect[Int]();
visible;

Lua tables also act as maps:

local ports = { web = 8080, admin = 9000 }
local port = ports.web

When absence matters, Musi examples return Option instead of relying on nil.

let option := import "@std/option";

let lookupPort (name : String) : option.Option[Int] :=
  match name (
  | "admin" => option.some[Int](9000)
  | "web" => option.some[Int](8080)
  | _ => option.none[Int]()
  );

lookupPort("web")
  |> option.unwrapOr[Int](3000);
summaryHtml: Use pipelines for sequence work and Option when keyed lookup may be absent.

title: Nil, Option, and Result
description: Translate Lua nil and multi-return failure conventions into Musi Option and Result values.
summary: Use Option for absence and Result for failure that carries information.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua nil and multi-return failure conventions into Musi Option and Result values.
html:
Nil, Option, and Result

Lua uses nil for absence, missing table fields, and many failure paths:

local ports = { admin = 9000 }
local port = ports.web
if port == nil then
  port = 8080
end

Musi uses the stdlib Option shape when a value may be absent.

let option := import "@std/option";

let lookupPort (name : String) : option.Option[Int] :=
  match name (
  | "admin" => option.some[Int](9000)
  | _ => option.none[Int]()
  );

let port := lookupPort("web")
  |> option.unwrapOr[Int](8080);
port;

Lua functions often return nil plus a message when failure carries information:

local function parsePort(text)
  if text == "8080" then
    return 8080
  end
  return nil, "invalid port"
end

Musi uses Result when callers should receive either a value or an error.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("invalid port")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;
summaryHtml: Use Option for absence and Result for failure that carries information.

title: Errors, Pcall, and Effects
description: Translate Lua error and pcall habits into Musi Result values and effect boundaries.
summary: Keep expected failure as data and outside work behind effectful APIs.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua error and pcall habits into Musi Result values and effect boundaries.
html:
Errors, Pcall, and Effects

Lua can raise errors and catch them with pcall:

local function parsePort(text)
  if text == "8080" then
    return 8080
  end
  error("parse error")
end

local ok, port = pcall(parsePort, "abc")
if not ok then
  port = 3000
end

Musi keeps expected failure in the returned value.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("parse error")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;

Lua host interaction often calls global or module functions directly:

io.write("name> ")
local name = io.read("*l")
print(name)

Musi keeps console work at effectful boundaries.

let io := import "@std/io";

let name := io.promptTrimmed("name> ");
io.writeLine(name);
summaryHtml: Keep expected failure as data and outside work behind effectful APIs.

title: Coroutines and Effect Boundaries
description: Translate Lua coroutine yield and resume habits into Musi effect requests and handlers.
summary: Use effects to name operations and handlers to decide how suspended work continues.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua coroutine yield and resume habits into Musi effect requests and handlers.
html:
Coroutines and Effect Boundaries

Lua coroutines can pause and resume a computation:

local worker = coroutine.create(function()
  coroutine.yield(8080)
  return 9000
end)

local ok, port = coroutine.resume(worker)

Musi names the operation as an effect request, then handles it at a boundary.

let PortSource := effect {
  let loadPort () : Int;
};

let loadPort () using { PortSource } : Int :=
  request PortSource.loadPort();

Handlers make the answer to an operation visible where the computation is interpreted.

let PortSource := effect {
  let loadPort () : Int;
};

handle PortSource.loadPort() using PortSource {
  value => value;
  loadPort(k) => resume 8080;
};
summaryHtml: Use effects to name operations and handlers to decide how suspended work continues.

title: Metatables, Classes, Instances, and Laws
description: Translate Lua metatable behavior into Musi classes, instances, and laws.
summary: Use classes for required behavior and laws for behavior expectations.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua metatable behavior into Musi classes, instances, and laws.
html:
Metatables, Classes, Instances, and Laws

Lua metatables let tables share behavior:

local Vehicle = {}
Vehicle.__index = Vehicle

function Vehicle:wheels()
  return 4
end

local car = setmetatable({}, Vehicle)

Musi classes state required operations. Instances attach those operations to a type.

let Vehicle[T] := class {
  let wheels(self : T) : Int;
  law atLeastFourWheels(vehicle : T) := vehicle.wheels() >= 4;
};

let Car := data {
  | Car
};

let carVehicle := instance Vehicle[Car] {
  let wheels(self : Car) : Int := 4;
};

A real-world analogy: a car is a vehicle, but road rules require at least four wheels for this category. The class names the operation. The law records the expectation implementations should satisfy.
summaryHtml: Use classes for required behavior and laws for behavior expectations.

title: Pattern Matching and Data Variants
description: Translate Lua tagged tables into Musi data variants and match patterns.
summary: Use variants when a value has one of several known shapes.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua tagged tables into Musi data variants and match patterns.
html:
Pattern Matching and Data Variants

Lua often represents alternatives with tagged tables:

local state = { tag = "running", id = 42 }

if state.tag == "running" then
  print(state.id)
end

Musi variants put the closed alternatives and payloads in one data definition.

let TaskState := data {
  | Waiting
  | Running(id : Int)
  | Done(code : Int)
};

let state : TaskState := .Running(id := 42);
match state (
| .Running(id) => id
| .Waiting => 0
| .Done(code) => code
);

match makes each case visible at the use site.
summaryHtml: Use variants when a value has one of several known shapes.

title: Modules, Imports, and Exports
description: Translate Lua require-return modules into Musi imports and exports.
summary: Use import and export directly to describe package-facing boundaries.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua require-return modules into Musi imports and exports.
html:
Modules, Imports, and Exports

Lua modules commonly return a table:

local Ports = {}

function Ports.defaultPort()
  return 8080
end

return Ports

Musi exports the package-facing value directly.

export let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

Lua imports another module with require:

local Ports = require("ports")
local port = Ports.defaultPort()

Musi imports a module value and calls through it.

let Ports := import "./ports";

let port := Ports.defaultPort();
port;
summaryHtml: Use import and export directly to describe package-facing boundaries.

title: Testing and Tooling
description: Translate Lua test habits into small Musi package tests and toolchain checks.
summary: Write tests as small examples and use the package toolchain for routine checks.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua test habits into small Musi package tests and toolchain checks.
html:
Testing and Tooling

Lua tests often call a function and raise an error when the expectation fails:

local function testDefaultPort()
  if defaultPort() ~= 8080 then
    error("default port mismatch")
  end
end

Musi tests keep the rule name, the small value, and the expectation together.

let testing := import "@std/testing";

testing.describe("ports");
testing.it("default port", testing.equal[Int](8080, 8080));
testing.endDescribe();

Use the package toolchain for formatting, checking, and running tests so local work matches CI behavior.
summaryHtml: Write tests as small examples and use the package toolchain for routine checks.

title: Embedding, C API, and FFI
description: Translate Lua embedding and C API boundaries into Musi foreign declarations, FFI types, and unsafe blocks.
summary: Keep ABI calls explicit and convert native shapes into ordinary Musi values near the boundary.
descriptionHtml: Translate Lua embedding and C API boundaries into Musi foreign declarations, FFI types, and unsafe blocks.
html:
Embedding, C API, and FFI

Lua is often embedded in a host program. C boundaries use the Lua C API or native functions:

local native = require("native_console")
native.puts("ready")

Musi declares the C ABI boundary with foreign "c" and calls it in an unsafe block.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

foreign "c" let puts (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt;

export let announce (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt :=
  unsafe { puts(message); };

Pointer helpers

Lua user data and host objects can wrap native memory. The raw memory boundary belongs near FFI code.

local pointer = native.nullPointer()
local isNull = pointer == nil

Musi keeps raw pointer work in @std/ffi helpers.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

let pointer := ffi.ptr.null[Int]();
let samePointer := unsafe { ffi.ptr.offset[Int](pointer, 0); };
ffi.ptr.isNull[Int](samePointer);
summaryHtml: Keep ABI calls explicit and convert native shapes into ordinary Musi values near the boundary.

title: Overview
description: Translate Python 3.14 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
summary: Start from Python habits, then read the equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python 3.14 habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
html:
Python 3.14 is the comparison point for this guide. Each page starts with Python code, then shows Musi code with the same names and the same job.

Python readers often rely on indentation, dynamic objects, None, exceptions, and flexible imports. Musi keeps the same practical goals, but makes value flow, data shape, and outside work visible in the code.

First translation

Python often starts with a small function and a result binding:

def total(base: int, fee: int) -> int:
    return base + fee

answer = total(1200, 45)
answer

Musi writes the same calculation as a let function. The final expression leaves the value.

let total (base : Int, fee : Int) : Int := base + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Read it like a kitchen ticket: inputs enter the named station, the expression at the end is the dish that leaves.

Reading path

  • Values, Functions, and Final Expressions
  • Blocks, Control Flow, and Repetition
  • Names, Mutation, and Fresh Values
  • Records, Objects, and Dictionaries
  • Collections and Pipelines
  • None, Option, and Result
  • Exceptions, Results, and Effects
  • Data Variants and Pattern Matching
  • Types, Generics, and Protocols
  • Modules and Packages
  • Testing and Tooling
  • Native Boundaries, Unsafe, and FFI

Python habits that transfer

  • name functions after domain work
  • keep data close to behavior that reads it
  • use tests as executable examples
  • make package boundaries clear
  • prefer readable code over clever compression

Python habits to translate

  • return becomes the final expression
  • indentation blocks become expression blocks with braces or parentheses
  • None becomes Option
  • exceptions become Result when failure is ordinary data
  • async and outside work become effect requests or stdlib runtime helpers
  • duck typing and Protocol become classes, instances, and laws when behavior needs rules
summaryHtml: Start from Python habits, then read the equivalent Musi expression, data, effect, and package shapes.

title: Values, Functions, and Final Expressions
description: Translate Python def, annotations, and return into Musi let functions and final expressions.
summary: Use let functions and final expressions where Python uses def and return.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python def, annotations, and return into Musi let functions and final expressions.
html:
Python def introduces a function. A type annotation helps readers and tools, but return still marks the value that leaves the function.

def total(base: int, fee: int) -> int:
    return base + fee

answer = total(1200, 45)
answer

Musi uses let for named values and named functions. The function body can be one expression.

let total (base : Int, fee : Int) : Int := base + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

The important habit change is where the result sits. Python says return base + fee; Musi lets base + fee be the body itself.

Named calls

Python keyword arguments help a call site read like a form:

def render(port: int, secure: bool) -> int:
    return port

selected = render(port=8080, secure=True)
selected

Musi supports the same readability with named arguments.

let render (port : Int, secure : Bool) : Int := port;

let selected := render(port := 8080, secure := 0 = 0);
selected;

Use named calls when position alone would make the call hard to read.
summaryHtml: Use let functions and final expressions where Python uses def and return.

title: Blocks, Control Flow, and Repetition
description: Translate Python indentation, loops, and branching habits into Musi expression blocks and recursion.
summary: Use blocks as expressions and let rec when repeated work needs a named path.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python indentation, loops, and branching habits into Musi expression blocks and recursion.
html:
Python indentation defines a block. A loop changes local state as it repeats work:

def total_seats(rounds: int) -> int:
    seats = 0
    remaining = rounds
    while remaining > 0:
        seats = seats + 4
        remaining = remaining - 1
    return seats

total_seats(3)

Musi gives the repeated path a name. let rec is the road sign that says the function may call itself.

let rec totalSeats (remaining : Int, seats : Int) : Int :=
  match remaining (
  | 0 => seats
  | _ => totalSeats(remaining - 1, seats + 4)
  );

let seats := totalSeats(3, 0);
seats;

Read the recursive version like a delivery route. One arm says the route is finished. The other arm makes the next stop with smaller remaining work.

Blocks return values

Python often builds a local value and returns it at the end of the function. Musi blocks can do the same job as one expression.

let invoiceTotal :=
  (
    let base := 1200;
    let fee := 45;
    base + fee
  );

invoiceTotal;

Use this when a calculation needs local names but outside code should see only the finished value.
summaryHtml: Use blocks as expressions and let rec when repeated work needs a named path.

title: Names, Mutation, and Fresh Values
description: Translate Python rebinding and mutable habits into Musi mutable values and fresh bindings.
summary: Use mut for real state changes and fresh names for ordinary derived values.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python rebinding and mutable habits into Musi mutable values and fresh bindings.
html:
Python lets a name point at a new value later:

visits = 0
visits = visits + 1

next_visits = visits + 1
next_visits

Musi makes real mutation visible at the value that can change.

let visits := mut 0;
visits := visits + 1;

let nextVisits := visits + 1;
nextVisits;

Think of mut as a counter on a desk. You can change the number on that counter, but readers can see which counter is allowed to move.

Fresh names first

When a step is just a derived value, prefer a new name:

base = 1200
total = base + 45
total

Musi reads that style directly.

let base := 1200;
let total := base + 45;
total;

Use mutation for counters, cursors, buffers, and small accumulators. Use fresh names when the next value is another fact in the calculation.
summaryHtml: Use mut for real state changes and fresh names for ordinary derived values.

title: Records, Objects, and Dictionaries
description: Translate Python dictionaries and dataclass-style data into Musi records and data definitions.
summary: Use records for named fields and data definitions when the shape deserves a name.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python dictionaries and dataclass-style data into Musi records and data definitions.
html:
Python dictionaries are quick named bags of data:

local = {
    "host": "localhost",
    "port": 8080,
    "secure": False,
}

public = {**local, "host": "api.example.com", "secure": True}
public["host"]

Musi records keep the same named-field idea. A data definition names the expected shape, and record spread updates selected fields.

let Endpoint := data {
  host : String;
  port : Int;
  secure : Bool;
};

let local := {
  host := "localhost",
  port := 8080,
  secure := 0 = 1
};

let public := { ...local, host := "api.example.com", secure := 0 = 0 };
public.host;

Use records like a shipping label: every field name tells the reader what the slot means.

Dataclass habit

Python dataclasses give a stable shape to object-like data. In Musi, a data definition is the stable shape; the record literal is the value that fills it.
summaryHtml: Use records for named fields and data definitions when the shape deserves a name.

title: Collections and Pipelines
description: Translate Python list operations into Musi arrays, stdlib helpers, and pipelines.
summary: Use arrays and pipeline-first stdlib helpers for visible collection flow.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python list operations into Musi arrays, stdlib helpers, and pipelines.

headings.text: Why this matters for Python readers

html:
Python list code often grows by applying one collection operation after another:

ports = [3000, 8080]
visible = [*ports, 9000]
visible

Musi arrays use []. The pipeline operator puts the value being transformed on the left and the operation on the right.

let iter := import "@std/iter";

let ports := [3000, 8080];
let visible := ports
  |> iter.append[Int](9000)
  |> iter.collect[Int]();
visible;

Read the pipeline like a conveyor belt: ports enters, iter.append adds a port, and iter.collect leaves an array for the next step.

Why this matters for Python readers

Python list comprehensions are compact. Musi favors a visible chain when multiple steps matter. That keeps later additions easy to review.
summaryHtml: Use arrays and pipeline-first stdlib helpers for visible collection flow.

title: None, Option, and Result
description: Translate Python None and ordinary failure values into Musi Option and Result.
summary: Use Option for maybe-present values and Result for ordinary success-or-error data.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python None and ordinary failure values into Musi Option and Result.
html:
Python commonly uses None when a value might be missing:

def lookup_port(name: str) -> int | None:
    match name:
        case "admin":
            return 9000
        case _:
            return None

port = lookup_port("web") or 8080
port

Musi uses Option so absence is visible in the type and at the call site.

let option := import "@std/option";

let lookupPort (name : String) : option.Option[Int] :=
  match name (
  | "admin" => option.some[Int](9000)
  | _ => option.none[Int]()
  );

let port := lookupPort("web")
  |> option.unwrapOr[Int](8080);
port;

The caller decides the fallback. The function only reports whether it found a value.

Result for ordinary failure

When Python returns a value or raises for ordinary input failure, Musi can model that as data.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("port must be numeric")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;

Use Option for missing value. Use Result when the error carries information a caller may read.
summaryHtml: Use Option for maybe-present values and Result for ordinary success-or-error data.

title: Exceptions, Results, and Effects
description: Translate Python exceptions and outside work into Musi Result values and effectful boundaries.
summary: Use Result for recoverable failure and effects or stdlib helpers for requested outside work.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python exceptions and outside work into Musi Result values and effectful boundaries.
html:
Python exceptions can carry a failure out of the ordinary return path:

def parse_port(text: str) -> int:
    try:
        return int(text)
    except ValueError:
        return 3000

port = parse_port("abc")
port

Musi keeps ordinary failure in a Result value. That makes the fallback decision visible.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsePort (text : String) : result.Result[Int, String] :=
  match text (
  | "8080" => result.ok[Int, String](8080)
  | _ => result.err[Int, String]("invalid port")
  );

let port := parsePort("abc")
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);
port;

Use this when failure is a normal part of the domain: bad input, missing config, or a rejected request.

Outside work

Python input, file access, time, and network calls reach outside the pure calculation. Musi keeps those edges explicit through stdlib modules and effects.

let io := import "@std/io";

let name := io.promptTrimmed("name> ");
io.writeLine(name);

The import tells readers where outside work comes from. The call site shows that the program is asking the runtime for input and output.
summaryHtml: Use Result for recoverable failure and effects or stdlib helpers for requested outside work.

title: Data Variants and Pattern Matching
description: Translate Python match and tagged object habits into Musi data variants and match arms.
summary: Use data variants when each case has a name and a different payload.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python match and tagged object habits into Musi data variants and match arms.
html:
Python match can branch on object shape, strings, tuples, or classes:

state = {"kind": "running", "id": 42}

match state:
    case {"kind": "running", "id": task_id}:
        selected = task_id
    case {"kind": "waiting"}:
        selected = 0
    case _:
        selected = -1

selected

Musi names the cases directly with data variants. The payload label says what travels with that case.

let TaskState := data {
  | Waiting
  | Running(id : Int)
  | Done(code : Int)
};

let state : TaskState := .Running(id := 42);
match state (
| .Running(id) => id
| .Waiting => 0
| .Done(code) => code
);

Use variants when a plain dictionary would make readers remember which string tags and fields are valid.
summaryHtml: Use data variants when each case has a name and a different payload.

title: Types, Generics, and Protocols
description: Translate Python annotations, generics, and Protocol habits into Musi type parameters, classes, and instances.
summary: Use generics for reusable value shape and classes for shared behavior.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python annotations, generics, and Protocol habits into Musi type parameters, classes, and instances.
html:
Python typing can describe a reusable function:

def identity[T](input: T) -> T:
    return input

port = identity[int](8080)
port

Musi type parameters sit beside the function name.

let identity[T] (input : T) : T := input;

let port := identity[Int](8080);
port;

The type parameter says the function works the same way for any T.

Protocol habits

Python Protocol names behavior that many types can satisfy:

from typing import Protocol

class Sized(Protocol):
    def size(self) -> int: ...

Musi uses a class for the behavior shape and an instance for a type that provides it.

let Sized[T] := class {
  let size(self : T) : Int;
};

let Crate := data {
  | Crate(items : Int)
};

let crateSized := instance Sized[Crate] {
  let size(self : Crate) : Int :=
    match self (
    | .Crate(items) => items
    );
};

Use a class when callers need behavior, not a particular concrete data type.
summaryHtml: Use generics for reusable value shape and classes for shared behavior.

title: Modules and Packages
description: Translate Python imports and package files into Musi exports, imports, and package paths.
summary: Use export for public names and import for package or relative module boundaries.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python imports and package files into Musi exports, imports, and package paths.
html:
Python modules export names by making them available at module scope:

# ports.py
def default_port() -> int:
    return 8080

Musi marks public names with export.

// ports.ms
export let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

Another file imports the module and calls the exported function:

import ports

port = ports.default_port()
port

Musi imports the module value and reads exported names from it.

let Ports := import "./ports";

let port := Ports.defaultPort();
port;

Package imports such as @std/io are for shared packages. Relative imports such as ./ports are for nearby project files.
summaryHtml: Use export for public names and import for package or relative module boundaries.

title: Testing and Tooling
description: Translate Python test habits into Musi std testing helpers and command-line checks.
summary: Use @std/testing to keep tests close to the behavior they describe.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python test habits into Musi std testing helpers and command-line checks.
html:
Python tests often name a small behavior and assert the result:

def default_port() -> int:
    return 8080

def test_default_port() -> None:
    assert default_port() == 8080

Musi uses @std/testing for named test suites and cases.

let testing := import "@std/testing";

let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

export let test () :=
  (
    testing.describe("ports");
    testing.it("default port is http alt", testing.toBe(defaultPort(), 8080));
    testing.endDescribe()
  );

A test should read like a receipt: name the behavior, run the check, and leave a pass or fail value.

Tooling habit

Python projects often separate formatting, type checks, and tests. Musi projects should do the same kind of loop with package checks and tests as the toolchain grows.
summaryHtml: Use @std/testing to keep tests close to the behavior they describe.

title: Native Boundaries, Unsafe, and FFI
description: Translate Python C extension and ctypes boundary habits into Musi foreign declarations, Ptr values, and unsafe blocks.
summary: Keep native calls explicit with foreign declarations, @std/ffi types, and unsafe blocks.
descriptionHtml: Translate Python C extension and ctypes boundary habits into Musi foreign declarations, Ptr values, and unsafe blocks.
html:
Python can cross into native code through extension modules, ctypes, or cffi. That boundary deserves a signpost because the host language cannot protect every operation there.

from ctypes import CDLL, c_char_p

libc = CDLL("libc.so.6")
puts = libc.puts
puts.argtypes = [c_char_p]
puts.restype = int

Musi declares the foreign function and keeps the call behind unsafe.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

foreign "c" let puts (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt;

export let announce (message : ffi.CString) : ffi.CInt :=
  unsafe { puts(message); };

foreign "c" says which native calling world the declaration belongs to. unsafe { ... } marks the operation that needs extra care.

Pointer helpers

Use @std/ffi pointer helpers when the native boundary gives you pointer-shaped values.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

let pointer := ffi.ptr.null[Int]();
let samePointer := unsafe { ffi.ptr.offset[Int](pointer, 0); };
ffi.ptr.isNull[Int](samePointer);

Keep raw native work small. Convert it to ordinary Musi data before passing values deeper into the program.
summaryHtml: Keep native calls explicit with foreign declarations, @std/ffi types, and unsafe blocks.

title: Overview
description: Translate Rust 2024 edition (1.85+) habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
summary: Start from Rust habits, then read the equivalent Musi expression, data, abstraction, effect, and FFI shapes.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust 2024 edition (1.85+) habits into Musi code with side-by-side examples.
html:
Rust 2024 edition (1.85+) is the comparison point for this guide. Each page starts with Rust code, then shows the Musi equivalent with the same names and the same job.

A Rust reader should not have to skim a generic Musi example and guess the translation. The Musi block below each Rust block is the translation.

First translation

Rust often starts with a small function and a binding for the result:

fn total(base: i32, fee: i32) -> i32 {
    base + fee
}

let answer = total(1200, 45);
answer

Musi writes the same calculation as a let function. The final expression leaves the value.

let total (base : Int, fee : Int) : Int := base + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

Reading path

Read these pages in order if you are moving a Rust habit into Musi code:

  • Values, Functions, and Final Expressions
  • Mutation
  • Records, Structs, and Field Updates
  • Enums, Data, and Pattern Matching
  • Traits, Classes, Instances, and Laws
  • Generics and Type Constructors
  • Results, Requests, and Effects
  • Modules, Packages, and Visibility
  • Unsafe and FFI
  • Testing and Tooling

Rust habits that transfer

  • name data by domain
  • keep functions small
  • make boundary work visible
  • use types to explain what values can be
  • test behavior close to the code that owns it

Rust habits to translate

  • let mut name becomes a binding to a mutable value
  • return is not used for ordinary final values
  • enum variants become data variants
  • trait and impl become class, instance, and laws when rules matter
  • Result handles recoverable data failure; effects handle requested outside work
  • raw native work stays behind foreign and unsafe
summaryHtml: Start from Rust habits, then read the equivalent Musi expression, data, abstraction, effect, and FFI shapes.

title: Values, Functions, and Final Expressions
description: Translate Rust bindings, functions, and final expressions into Musi.
summary: Rust `let`, `fn`, and final-expression habits map to Musi `let` functions and expression results.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust bindings, functions, and final expressions into Musi.
html:
Rust functions commonly end with an expression instead of an explicit return:

fn total(base: i32, fee: i32) -> i32 {
    base + fee
}

let answer = total(1200, 45);
answer

Musi uses the same expression-first idea, but function definitions are let bindings with parameters.

let total (base : Int, fee : Int) : Int := base + fee;

let answer := total(1200, 45);
answer;

total is a name bound to a function. answer is a name bound to the call result. The last expression is the value of the block or file.

Named arguments

Rust readers often use struct fields or builder methods when positional calls become unclear. Musi can name call arguments directly:

fn render(port: u16, secure: bool) -> u16 {
    port
}

let selected = render(8080, true);
selected

The Musi equivalent can keep positional order or spell labels at the call site.

let render (port : Int, secure : Bool) : Int := port;

let selected := render(port := 8080, secure := 0 = 0);
selected;

Use named arguments when two values have the same type or when swapped order would be easy to miss.
summaryHtml: Rust let, fn, and final-expression habits map to Musi let functions and expression results.

title: Mutation
description: Translate Rust 2024 edition (1.85+) mutation habits into Musi's value-based mutation model.
summary: Read Musi mutation as a mutable value binding, not as a variable-mode prefix.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust 2024 edition (1.85+) mutation habits into Musi's value-based mutation model.
html:
Rust marks the binding mutable:

let mut visits = 0;
visits += 1;
visits

Musi keeps mutability on the value owned by the binding.

let visits := mut 0;
visits := visits + 1;
visits;

Read let visits := mut 0; as a normal name bound to a value that may change. The update writes the next value back into that mutable value.

When to translate let mut

Use Musi mutation for Rust code where the same local state changes over time:

  • counters
  • parser cursors
  • reusable buffers
  • local totals built over several steps

If Rust uses mut only to stage a clearer next value, Musi often reads better with a new let name.

let base = 1200;
let total = base + 45;
total
let base := 1200;
let total := base + 45;
total;
summaryHtml: Read Musi mutation as a mutable value binding, not as a variable-mode prefix.

title: Records, Structs, and Field Updates
description: Translate Rust structs and update syntax into Musi records.
summary: Rust structs map to named Musi record shapes and spread updates.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust structs and update syntax into Musi records.
html:
Rust structs group named fields:

struct Endpoint {
    host: String,
    port: u16,
    secure: bool,
}

let local = Endpoint {
    host: String::from("localhost"),
    port: 8080,
    secure: false,
};

let secure = Endpoint {
    secure: true,
    ..local
};

secure.port

Musi uses record-shaped data for the field contract and record literals for values.

let Endpoint := data {
  host : String;
  port : Int;
  secure : Bool;
};

let local := {
  host := "localhost",
  port := 8080,
  secure := 0 = 1
};

let secure := { ...local, secure := 0 = 0 };
secure.port;

The spread update starts from local, then replaces only secure. Field access stays direct: secure.port.
summaryHtml: Rust structs map to named Musi record shapes and spread updates.

title: Enums, Data, and Pattern Matching
description: Translate Rust enums with payloads into Musi data variants and matches.
summary: Rust enum variants map to Musi `data` variants with named payloads and `match` arms.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust enums with payloads into Musi data variants and matches.
html:
Rust enum variants can carry named payloads:

enum Port {
    Configured { port: u16 },
    Default,
}

let selected = Port::Configured { port: 8080 };
match selected {
    Port::Configured { port } => port,
    Port::Default => 3000,
}

Musi puts payload labels inside constructor-style variant declarations.

let Port := data {
  | Configured(port : Int)
  | Default
};

let selected : Port := .Configured(port := 8080);
match selected (
| .Configured(port) => port
| .Default => 3000
);

Constructor calls use named payloads. Match arms read the value back through the variant shape.
summaryHtml: Rust enum variants map to Musi data variants with named payloads and match arms.

title: Traits, Classes, Instances, and Laws
description: Translate Rust traits and impls into Musi classes, instances, and laws.
summary: Rust trait contracts map to Musi classes, instances, and explicit laws when behavior has rules.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust traits and impls into Musi classes, instances, and laws.
html:
Rust traits name shared behavior. Implementations attach that behavior to a type:

trait Vehicle {
    fn wheels(&self) -> usize;

    fn at_least_four_wheels(&self) -> bool {
        self.wheels() >= 4
    }
}

struct Car;

impl Vehicle for Car {
    fn wheels(&self) -> usize {
        4
    }
}

Musi separates the shape from the instance and writes the rule as a law.

let Vehicle[T] := class {
  let wheels(self : T) : Int;
  law atLeastFourWheels(vehicle : T) := vehicle.wheels() >= 4;
};

let Car := data {
  | Car
};

let carVehicle := instance Vehicle[Car] {
  let wheels(self : Car) : Int := 4;
};

A car is a vehicle, but the law says which vehicle instances count as valid in this model. The class names behavior. The instance provides behavior. The law names the expectation readers can rely on.
summaryHtml: Rust trait contracts map to Musi classes, instances, and explicit laws when behavior has rules.

title: Generics and Type Constructors
description: Translate Rust generic functions and generic data into Musi type parameters.
summary: Rust generic functions and structs map to Musi bracketed type parameters and constructor-style data.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust generic functions and generic data into Musi type parameters.
html:
Rust generic functions name the type parameter before the function parameters:

fn identity<T>(input: T) -> T {
    input
}

let port = identity::<u16>(8080);
port

Musi uses bracketed type parameters on the let function.

let identity[T] (input : T) : T := input;

let port := identity[Int](8080);
port;

Generic data follows the same bracketed shape:

struct Box1<T> {
    value: T,
}

let boxed = Box1 { value: 8080 };
boxed.value
let Box1[T] := data {
  | Box1(value : T)
};

let boxed := .Box1(value := 8080);
match boxed (
| .Box1\(value) => value
);

The Musi match reads the payload back out through the constructor shape.

When a class receives a type constructor, Musi can name that constructor in the type parameter list.

let Box1[T] := data {
  | Box1(value : T)
};

let Keeps[F : Type -> Type] := class {
  let keep(value : F[Int]) : F[Int];
};

let boxKeeps := instance Keeps[Box1] {
  let keep(value : Box1[Int]) : Box1[Int] := value;
};
summaryHtml: Rust generic functions and structs map to Musi bracketed type parameters and constructor-style data.

title: Results, I/O, and Effects
description: Translate Rust `Result` and I/O boundary habits into Musi stdlib results and I/O.
summary: Rust `Result` maps to `@std/result`; standard input starts with `@std/io`.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust Result and I/O boundary habits into Musi stdlib results and I/O.
html:
Rust often uses Result for recoverable failure:

fn read_port(text: &str) -> Result<u16, String> {
    text.parse::<u16>().map_err(|_| String::from("bad port"))
}

let parsed = Ok(8080);
parsed

Musi uses @std/result for the same recoverable shape.

let result := import "@std/result";

let parsed : result.Result[Int, String] := result.ok[Int, String](8080);
parsed
  |> result.unwrapOr[Int, String](3000);

That keeps recoverable failure in the value, with helpers for fallback or mapping.

Rust also uses Result for outside work such as reading from standard input:

fn read_line() -> std::result::Result<String, std::io::Error> {
    let mut line = String::new();
    std::io::stdin().read_line(&mut line)?;
    Ok(line)
}

Musi user code reaches for @std/io first. The runtime request stays under that wrapper.

let io := import "@std/io";

let line := io.readTrimmedLine();
line;

Use stdlib results when the value itself can be one of several outcomes. Use stdlib I/O for ordinary terminal boundaries. Define custom effects when the program needs a capability that is not already in the standard library.
summaryHtml: Rust Result maps to @std/result; standard input starts with @std/io.

title: Modules, Packages, and Visibility
description: Translate Rust module exports and package calls into Musi imports and exports.
summary: Rust `pub mod` habits map to Musi exported values and imported packages.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust module exports and package calls into Musi imports and exports.
html:
Rust module code often exposes a small public function:

pub mod ports {
    pub fn default_port() -> u16 {
        8080
    }
}

let port = ports::default_port();
port

Musi exports names from a file or package and imports that package where the names are needed.

export let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

A consumer reads the exported name through the imported package value.

let Ports := import "./ports";

let port := Ports.defaultPort();
port;

Keep exports small. Export names a reader should depend on; leave local helpers unexported.
summaryHtml: Rust pub mod habits map to Musi exported values and imported packages.

title: Unsafe and FFI
description: Translate Rust unsafe and extern C habits into Musi foreign declarations and unsafe blocks.
summary: Rust `extern` and raw pointer work maps to Musi `foreign`, `CPtr`, `@std/ffi`, and `unsafe`.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust unsafe and extern C habits into Musi foreign declarations and unsafe blocks.
html:
Rust marks C ABI declarations and raw pointer calls explicitly:

unsafe extern "C" {
    fn get_counter() -> *mut i32;
}

let counter = unsafe { get_counter() };

Musi keeps the same boundary visible with foreign and unsafe.

let ffi := import "@std/ffi";

foreign "c" let get_counter () : CPtr;

let counter := unsafe {
  ffi.ptr.cast[Int](get_counter());
};

Use foreign for the ABI declaration. Use unsafe for the block where raw native assumptions matter. Use @std/ffi helpers when pointer views need typed reads, writes, offsets, or casts.
summaryHtml: Rust extern and raw pointer work maps to Musi foreign, CPtr, @std/ffi, and unsafe.

title: Testing and Tooling
description: Translate Rust unit-test habits into Musi package tests.
summary: Rust `#[test]` habits map to Musi package tests with `@std/testing`.
descriptionHtml: Translate Rust unit-test habits into Musi package tests.
html:
Rust usually keeps a small test beside the code that owns the behavior:

fn default_port() -> u16 {
    8080
}

#[test]
fn default_port_is_http_alt() {
    assert_eq!(default_port(), 8080);
}

Musi writes a test function and uses @std/testing assertions.

let testing := import "@std/testing";

let defaultPort () : Int := 8080;

export let test () :=
  (
    testing.describe("ports");
    testing.it("default port is http alt", testing.toBe(defaultPort(), 8080));
    testing.endDescribe()
  );

A package test should name behavior, call the function under test, and return the test runner's result expression.
summaryHtml: Rust #[test] habits map to Musi package tests with @std/testing.
